# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Part (i) – Sizing Processes: Hazards & Control

### 2.1.1 What is Sizing?

**Sizing** is the process of applying a protective film (size) – usually starch, PVA, or CMC – onto warp yarns to improve their strength, abrasion resistance, and smoothness during weaving.

### 2.1.2 Sizing Process Flow

The sizing process follows this sequence:
1. **Size mixing** (starch + water + additives)
2. **Cooking** in a pressure vessel (size kettle) at **~100–150 °C**
3. **Transport** of cooked size via pipelines to the sizing machine
4. **Application** to warp yarns on the slasher
5. **Drying** on steam-heated cylinders

### 2.1.3 Hazard Inventory at the Sizing Section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Examiners love asking "List the hazards at the size kitchen." Memorize the table below.

| # | Hazard | Source | Type of Injury | Control Measure |
|---|--------|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Steam burns** | Open valves, leaking pipes | 1st–3rd degree burns | Insulated piping, valve guards |
| 2 | **Boiling starch splashes** | Stirring, opening kettles | Scalds | Splash guards, PPE (face shield) |
| 3 | **Pressure explosion** | Size kettle overpressure | Catastrophic fatality | Pressure relief valves (PRV), rupture discs |
| 4 | **Chemical burns** | Caustic additives, acids | Skin/eye damage | Chemical-resistant gloves, eyewash stations |
| 5 | **Slip and fall** | Wet starch on floor | Fractures, sprains | Anti-slip flooring, drainage |
| 6 | **Hot cylinder contact** | Steam-heated drying cylinders | Contact burns | Cylinder guards, warning signs |
| 7 | **Manual handling** | Carrying size bags | Back injuries | Mechanical lifting aids |
| 8 | **Fire from size dust** | Dry starch accumulation | Flash fire | Housekeeping, dust extraction |

### 2.1.4 Cooking Vessel (Size Kettle) – Detailed Hazard Analysis

A **size cooking kettle** is essentially a jacketed pressure vessel where saturated steam (typically at **2–4 bar gauge pressure**) heats a starch-water slurry.

**Critical hazard zones:**
- **Pressure zone:** Risk of explosion if PRV fails
- **Temperature zone:** Internal slurry at **~95–105 °C**
- **Steam jacket zone:** Surface temperature of **~140–160 °C**
- **Agitator zone:** Entanglement risk at the drive shaft

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Truth:** The most common accident in size kitchens is **steam burn from valve opening**, not kettle explosion. This is because steam is invisible until it condenses on the skin, causing severe burns.

### 2.1.5 Transport of Size – Pipeline Hazards

Cooked size is transported through **insulated pipelines** from the kettle to the slasher sizing box. Hazards include:
- **Blockage** leading to pressure build-up
- **Burns at flanges** and joints
- **Insulation degradation** releasing fibers

### 2.1.6 Hazards Due to Steam – Engineering Details

Steam is the most hazardous utility in a textile mill. A single kg of steam at **2 bar (g)** releases latent heat of **~2206 kJ** upon condensation.

**Steam Burn Severity Equation:**
$$
\text{Burn Depth} \propto T_{\text{steam}} \times t_{\text{contact}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{steam}}$ = Steam temperature (°C or K)
- $t_{\text{contact}}$ = Contact time (seconds)

**Key KTU Formulas:**

$$
Q = m \cdot L_v
$$

$$
P \cdot V = m \cdot R_{\text{specific}} \cdot T
$$

$$
\text{Saturation Pressure: } \log_{10}(P) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}
$$

Where for water/steam: $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$ (T in °C, P in mm Hg).

---

## 2.2 Part (ii) – Loom Shed Hazards

### 2.2.1 Loom Shed Definition

A **loom shed** is the dedicated building or section of a weaving mill housing weaving machines (looms). It is the largest production area in a weaving mill and contains the highest density of moving machinery.

### 2.2.2 Shuttle Looms – Hazard Inventory

A **shuttle loom** uses a projectile (shuttle) to carry the weft yarn across the warp shed. Hazards include:

| # | Hazard | Mechanism | Severity |
|---|--------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **Shuttle fly-off** | Shuttle exits shed at 20–25 m/s | Fatal head/eye injury |
| 2 | **Belt entanglement** | Open drive belts | Amputation |
| 3 | **Beat-up arm impact** | Reed swing | Crush injury |
| 4 | **Warp break entanglement** | Fast-moving yarn | Hand/finger laceration |
| 5 | **Noise (>95 dB)** | Mechanical impacts | Permanent hearing loss |
| 6 | **Lubricant slip** | Oil on floor | Falls |
| 7 | **Take-up roll crush** | Rotating take-up | Hand entrapment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Shuttle fly-off was historically the single most fatal loom accident before **shuttleless loom adoption** and **anti-shuttle-fly guards** (Bowden wire or photoelectric barriers).

### 2.2.3 Shuttleless Looms – Hazard Inventory

Shuttleless looms (rapier, projectile, air-jet, water-jet) eliminate the shuttle but introduce new hazards:

| Loom Type | Primary Hazards |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **Rapier loom** | Rapier head entanglement, gripper failure |
| **Projectile loom** | Projectile fly-off (similar to shuttle), gripper bar |
| **Air-jet loom** | Compressed air release, high noise (100+ dB), eye injury from air |
| **Water-jet loom** | Water injection burns, electrical short, slip hazard |

### 2.2.4 Common Loom Shed Hazards (All Looms)

1. **Mechanical entanglement** – gears, belts, cams, camshafts
2. **Crush points** – between moving parts (e.g., reed and breast beam)
3. **Shear points** – cutting edges of reeds, knives
4. **Stored energy** – picking mechanism, warp beam
5. **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)** – continuous **90–105 dB(A)**
6. **Vibration** – whole-body vibration from heavy beams
7. **Dust** – fly/fiber liberation
8. **Poor lighting** – eyestrain, weaving mistakes
9. **Heat stress** – multiple heat sources (electric motors)
10. **Electrical hazards** – 3-phase motors, switchgear

---

## 2.3 Part (iii) – Knitting Machine Hazards

### 2.3.1 Knitting Process Overview

Knitting forms fabric by interlooping yarns using needles. Two main categories:
- **Weft knitting** (circular, flat knitting machines)
- **Warp knitting** (tricot, raschel machines)

### 2.3.2 Knitting Machine Hazard Matrix

| Machine Type | Hazard | Specific Risk |
|--------------|--------|---------------|
| Circular knitting | Needle penetration | Fingers into needle cylinder |
| Flat knitting | Carriage return | Crush at edges |
| Warp knitting | Latch needle breakage | High-velocity ejection |
| Hosiery | Toe closing seam | Needle bar impact |
| Computerized flat | Yarn carrier movement | Pinch points |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse "warp knitting" with "warp sizing." Remember – warp knitting is a fabric formation process using multiple warp yarns fed simultaneously, while warp sizing prepares warp yarn for weaving.

### 2.3.3 Specific Needle Hazards

The needles in knitting machines are extremely sharp and move at high frequencies:
- **Circular knitting machine:** Cylinder rotates at **20–30 rpm**, with **~2000–4000 needles** in the cylinder. Total needle encounters per minute can exceed **60,000**.
- **Flat knitting machine:** Carriage traverses at **1–2 m/s**.

The primary hazards:
1. **Needle-stick injuries** (often to fingers during yarn threading)
2. **Entanglement** in yarn feed or take-up rollers
3. **Crush injuries** at the cylinder/dial interface (circular knitters)
4. **Lubricant exposure** (oil mist dermatitis)

---

## 2.4 Part (iv) – Non-Woven Hazards

### 2.4.1 Non-Woven Manufacturing Definition

**Non-wovens** are engineered fabrics produced directly from fibers (or filaments) without weaving or knitting. The bond is created by:
- **Mechanical** (needle-punching, hydroentanglement, stitch-bonding)
- **Thermal** (calendering, through-air bonding)
- **Chemical** (binder application)

### 2.4.2 Non-Woven Section Hazards

| Process | Primary Hazard | Secondary Hazard |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|
| Fiber opening/blending | Dust explosion, entanglement | Noise, vibration |
| Carding | Licker-in roller entanglement | Static electricity |
| Needle-punching | Needle bar failure (projectile) | High-frequency noise |
| Hydroentanglement | High-pressure water injection | Slip, electrical |
| Spunbonding | Hot polymer extrusion | Burns, fume inhalation |
| Meltblowing | Ultra-fine fiber inhalation | Respiratory issues |
| Chemical bonding | Solvent/binder exposure | Fire, dermatitis |
| Thermal bonding | Heated calender rolls | Contact burns |
| Web handling | Roll crush | Manual handling |

### 2.4.3 Dust Explosion Hazard (Critical for Non-Woven)

Fiber dust in non-woven sections can form explosive atmospheres:
- **Minimum Explosive Concentration (MEC)** for cotton: **~50 g/m³**
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE):** **~10–25 mJ**
- **Kst value (cotton dust):** ~**80 bar·m/s** (St Class 1)

$$
\text{Explosion Pressure: } P_{\max} = P_0 + K_{st} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{V_0}\right)^{1/3}
$$

### 2.4.4 Needle-Punching Specific Hazards

Needle-punching machines operate with **thousands of barbed needles** mounted on a board that reciprocates at **800–2000 strokes/min**. Each needle can reach **~40 m/s** velocity.

**Hazards:**
- **Needle ejection** if boards are not properly clamped
- **Fiber fly** causing respiratory irritation
- **Mechanical entanglement** at feed and take-up
- **High noise** at **~95–100 dB(A)**
- **Vibration** affecting structural integrity of the building

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $Q = m \cdot L_v$ | Heat released by condensing steam | Q=heat, m=mass, $L_v$=latent heat | J, kg, J/kg |
| $P V = n R T$ | Ideal gas law for steam | P=pressure, V=volume, T=temp | Pa, m³, K |
| $T_{\text{skin burn}} = f(P, t)$ | Steam burn severity relation | Steam pressure & time | bar, s |
| $L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Equivalent noise level | $L_i$=level, $t_i$=time | dB(A), s |
| $P_{\max} = P_0 + K_{st}(V/V_0)^{1/3}$ | Dust explosion pressure | $K_{st}$=explosion index | bar, m³ |
| $E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Projectile (shuttle) kinetic energy | m=mass, v=velocity | J, kg, m/s |
| $\text{Burn Injury} \propto T_{\text{steam}} \cdot t_{\text{contact}}$ | Burn depth relation | Temperature and time | K, s |
| $\text{Pressure Relief Area: } A = \frac{W}{K \cdot P_{\text{set}}}$ | API 520 PRV sizing | W=mass flow, K=coefficient | mm², bar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The shuttle of a conventional loom at **20 m/s** with mass **~250 g** carries kinetic energy of **50 J** – enough to be lethal. This is why modern mills prefer shuttleless looms, not just for speed but for safety.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinetic Energy of a Flying Shuttle

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A shuttle of mass **0.25 kg** is propelled across the loom shed at **22 m/s**. Calculate the kinetic energy and assess the safety implications if it strikes a worker.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the kinetic energy formula**

$$
E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values**

$$
E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.25 \cdot (22)^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the velocity squared**

$$
E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0.25 \cdot 484
$$

**Step 4: Compute the product**

$$
E_{\text{kinetic}} = 0.125 \cdot 484 = 60.5 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result**

$$
E_{\text{kinetic}} = 60.5 \text{ J}
$$

This energy level exceeds the threshold for penetrating skull bone (~**40–60 J**), confirming that a shuttle fly-off is potentially **fatal**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steam Burn Severity Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Saturated steam at **2 bar (g)** escapes and contacts a worker's forearm for **3 seconds**. Estimate the burn severity given the surface heat flux.

**Step 1: Saturated steam temperature at 2 bar(g)**

At 2 bar gauge (= 3 bar absolute), the saturation temperature from steam tables is approximately:

$$
T_{\text{sat}} = 143.6 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2: Heat transferred to skin (Newton's law of cooling)**

$$
Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t
$$

**Step 3: Assume $h = 2500 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ for condensing steam on skin, $A = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$, $\Delta T = 143.6 - 37 = 106.6 \text{ °C}$, $t = 3 \text{ s}$**

$$
Q = 2500 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 106.6 \cdot 3
$$

**Step 4: Compute the result**

$$
Q = 2500 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 106.6 \cdot 3 = 7995 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to skin damage threshold**

Skin destruction begins at approximately **$Q = 50 \text{ kJ/m}^2$**. For $A = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
Q_{\text{threshold}} = 50{,}000 \cdot 0.01 = 500 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6: Compare**

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = 7995 \text{ J} > Q_{\text{threshold}} = 500 \text{ J}
$$

This implies **second or third-degree burns** – a real-life severe injury.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Noise Level in Loom Shed

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A weaver works in a loom shed for **8 hours**. The noise level is **95 dB(A)** for **6 hours** and **85 dB(A)** for **2 hours**. Calculate the equivalent continuous noise level $L_{eq}$.

**Step 1: State the formula**

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{8} \left[6 \cdot 10^{95/10} + 2 \cdot 10^{85/10}\right]\right)
$$

**Step 3: Compute the exponentials**

$$
10^{95/10} = 10^{9.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by time**

$$
6 \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{9} = 1.897 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
2 \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{8} = 6.324 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 5: Sum and divide**

$$
\frac{1.897 \times 10^{10} + 6.324 \times 10^{8}}{8} = \frac{1.960 \times 10^{10}}{8} = 2.450 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the log**

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(2.450 \times 10^{9}) = 10 \cdot 9.389 = 93.89 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

**Step 7: Conclusion**

$$
L_{eq} \approx 93.9 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

This **exceeds** the OSHA permissible exposure limit of **90 dB(A) for 8 hours**, mandating a hearing conservation program.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard Severity vs. Probability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** This matrix is often asked in 7-mark questions to "rank hazards by risk."

| Hazard | Probability | Severity | Risk Rating |
|--------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| Shuttle fly-off | Low (with guards) | Catastrophic | **Medium-High** |
| Steam burn at valve | Medium | Serious | **High** |
| Needle stick in knitting | Medium | Minor–Moderate | **Medium** |
| Dust explosion in non-woven | Low | Catastrophic | **High** |
| Noise-induced hearing loss | High | Moderate–Serious | **High** |
| Belt entanglement | Medium | Serious–Catastrophic | **High** |
| Chemical splash | Low | Moderate | **Low–Medium** |
| Slip on wet floor | High | Moderate | **High** |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) Sizing for a Size Kettle

### 3.5.1 Problem Statement

A size kettle has a heating capacity of **500 kW** with steam at **3 bar(g)**. Determine the required PRV orifice area using API 520 simplified method.

**Step 1: Determine the heat input rate**

$$
\dot{Q} = 500{,}000 \text{ W} = 500 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Convert heat to steam generation rate**

Using the latent heat of steam at 3 bar(g), $L_v \approx 2137 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{L_v} = \frac{500}{2137} = 0.234 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3: Apply API 520 orifice equation**

$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}}{K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_{\text{set}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{Z \cdot T}}}
$$

**Step 4: Use simplified saturated steam coefficient**

For saturated steam relief at $P_{\text{set}}$ in bar(g):

$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}}{K \cdot P_{\text{set}}}
$$

With $K = 0.7$ (typical for steam), $P_{\text{set}} = 3 + 1.1 = 4.1 \text{ bar(g)}$ (10% accumulation):

$$
A = \frac{0.234}{0.7 \cdot 4.1 \times 10^5} \text{ (in m}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 5: Convert**

$$
A = \frac{0.234}{2.87 \times 10^5} = 8.15 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Convert to mm²**

$$
A = 0.815 \text{ mm}^2
$$

This is impractical – so we conclude the PRV need not be sized for total heat input since fire is the worst case. For fire case, multiply by **fire condition factor** typically **0.5–1.0**:

$$
A_{\text{fire}} = \frac{0.234 \cdot 0.5}{0.7 \cdot 4.1 \times 10^5} \approx 0.41 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Still small – this is a typical PRV size of **1/2"–3/4"** inlet for such kettles.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Dust Explosion Pressure in Non-Woven Section

### 3.6.1 Problem Statement

A 50 m³ non-woven blending room contains cotton fiber dust at 100 g/m³. The $K_{st}$ value is 80 bar·m/s. Find the maximum explosion pressure.

**Step 1: State the equation**

$$
P_{\max} = P_0 + K_{st} \cdot \left(\frac{V}{V_0}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2: With reference volume $V_0 = 1 \text{ m}^3$ and $P_0 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$**

$$
P_{\max} = 1.013 + 80 \cdot \left(\frac{50}{1}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3: Compute cube root**

$$
50^{1/3} \approx 3.684
$$

**Step 4: Final calculation**

$$
P_{\max} = 1.013 + 80 \cdot 3.684 = 1.013 + 294.7 = 295.7 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 5: Practical implication**

This enormous theoretical pressure is why **explosion relief panels** (venting area ~**0.1–0.2 m² per 100 m³** of room) are mandatory.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Hazard Control Selection Matrix

| Hazard | Eliminate | Substitute | Engineering Control | Admin Control | PPE |
|--------|-----------|------------|---------------------|---------------|-----|
| Shuttle fly-off | Use shuttleless | Use projectile with guard | Anti-fly guards, photoelectric | Training | Steel toe boots, hard hat |
| Steam burns | None | Low-pressure steam | Insulation, steam traps | Lock-out/tag-out | Heat-resistant gloves |
| Needle stick | None | N/A | Needle guards, light curtains | Training | Cut-resistant gloves |
| Dust explosion | None | Water-based process | Explosion vents, dust extraction | Housekeeping | Dust masks |
| Noise | Electric drives | Quieter looms | Acoustic enclosures | Rotation | Ear muffs/plugs |
| Chemical splash | None | Less hazardous size | Splash guards, eyewash | SOPs | Goggles, apron |

```mermaid
    N1[Fiber Bale Opening] --> N2[Blending]
    N2 --> N3[Carding]
    N3 --> N4[Web Formation]
    N4 --> N5A[Needle Punching]
    N4 --> N5B[Hydroentanglement]
    N4 --> N5C[Thermal Bonding]
    N4 --> N5D[Chemical Bonding]
    N5A --> N6[Calendering]
    N5B --> N6
    N5C --> N6
    N5D --> N6
    N6 --> N7[Winding]

    N2 -.-> NH1[Dust Explosion]
    N3 -.-> NH2[Licker-in Entanglement]
    N5A -.-> NH3[Needle Bar Failure]
    N5B -.-> NH4[High-Pressure Water Injection]
    N5C -.-> NH5[Hot Roller Contact]
    N5D -.-> NH6[Chemical Vapor Exposure]
    N6 -.-> NH7[Roll Crush]

    NH1 --> NS1[Explosion Vents, Dust Extraction]
    NH2 --> NS2[Licker-in Guard]
    NH3 --> NS3[Clamp Inspection, Vibration Check]
    NH4 --> NS4[Pressure Lockout, Wet Floor Signs]
    NH5 --> NS5[Calender Guard, Heat PPE]
    NH6 --> NS6[Ventilation, Respirator]
    NH7 --> NS7[Roll Stoppers, Two-Hand Control]
```

## 4.6 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Loom Shed Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Loom Shed Noise 95-105 dB] --> H1A[Eliminate Use Electric Looms]
    H1A --> H1B[Substitute Quieter Loom Type]
    H1B --> H1C[Engineering Acoustic Enclosure]
    H1C --> H1D[Administrative Job Rotation]
    H1D --> H1E[PPE Ear Muffs and Plugs]
    H1E --> H1F[Target Below 85 dB Exposure]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "accident hazard" in a textile mill. List four primary hazard categories.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
An **accident hazard** is any condition, substance, or activity in a textile mill that has the intrinsic potential to cause harm to personnel, property, or the environment. The four primary hazard categories are:
1. **Physical hazards** – noise, heat, moving machinery
2. **Chemical hazards** – sizes, dyes, solvents
3. **Biological hazards** – cotton dust (byssinosis)
4. **Ergonomic hazards** – repetitive motion, awkward postures

### Question 2: Why is steam considered one of the most dangerous utilities in a textile sizing section?
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Steam is dangerous because:
1. It carries enormous **latent heat** (~2206 kJ/kg at 100 °C), causing severe burns on contact.
2. It is **invisible**, making leaks hard to detect.
3. It operates under **pressure**, posing explosion risk.
4. Steam burns are typically **second or third degree** because condensation releases heat continuously on the skin.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the accident hazards associated with sizing processes, including cooking vessels, transport of size, and hazards due to steam. Suggest suitable control measures.**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hazards at the size cooking vessel:**
- **Pressure explosion** if PRV fails or is undersized
- **Steam burns** at valves and joints
- **Boiling starch scalds** during kettle opening
- **Agitator entanglement** at drive shaft
- **Chemical burns** from caustic additives

**Control measures:**
- Install calibrated **pressure relief valves** and rupture discs
- Provide **insulated jackets** on all steam piping
- Install **splash guards** at kettle openings
- Cover agitator shafts with **fixed guards**
- Train workers on **lock-out/tag-out (LOTO)** procedures

**(ii) Hazards during transport of size:**
- **Pipeline blockage** causing pressure build-up
- **Insulation degradation** exposing hot surfaces
- **Leakage at flanges** causing slip hazards

**Control measures:**
- Install **pressure gauges** at inlet and outlet of pipelines
- Conduct **periodic insulation inspections**
- Use **proper flange guards** and drip trays

**(iii) Hazards due to steam:**
- **Steam burns** at valve openings
- **Steam hammer** in pipelines
- **Condensate-induced water hammer** causing pipe rupture

**Control measures:**
- Install **steam traps** to remove condensate
- Use **insulated valves** and **automatic steam shut-off** systems
- Provide **PPE**: heat-resistant gloves, face shields, aprons

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Explain the various hazards in a loom shed operating both shuttle and shuttleless looms. What engineering controls are recommended?**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Shuttle loom hazards:**
- **Shuttle fly-off:** The shuttle at 20–25 m/s can fly out of the shed, causing fatal injuries
- **Belt entanglement:** Open drive belts can entangle loose clothing
- **Beat-up injury:** The reed moves forcefully at each pick
- **Noise:** Continuous exposure to 90–100 dB(A)
- **Warp break entanglement:** Loose threads may catch fingers

**Shuttleless loom hazards:**
- **Rapier looms:** Rapier head entanglement, gripper failure
- **Air-jet looms:** Compressed air injection, noise (100+ dB)
- **Water-jet looms:** Water burn risk, electrical short, slip hazard
- **Projectile looms:** Projectile fly-off (similar to shuttle)

**Engineering controls:**
1. **Anti-shuttle-fly guards** (Bowden wire, photoelectric barriers)
2. **Fixed machine guards** on belts, gears, camshafts
3. **Acoustic enclosures** around looms
4. **Two-hand control** for starting looms
5. **Light curtains** at operator stations
6. **Emergency stop buttons** within reach
7. **Proper housekeeping** – oil spillage control, lint removal

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) – 7 Marks
**Describe the hazards associated with knitting machines. What specific guards and PPE are recommended for circular and flat knitting machines?**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Knitting machine hazards:**

1. **Needle-stick injuries:**
   - The most common injury in knitting
   - Circular machines have **2000–4000 needles** rotating at 20–30 rpm
   - Fingers may contact needles during yarn threading or piecing-up

2. **Crush injuries:**
   - At the **cylinder/dial interface** in circular knitting
   - At the **carriage edges** in flat knitting

3. **Entanglement:**
   - In yarn feed mechanisms
   - In take-down rollers

4. **Lubricant exposure:**
   - Oil mist dermatitis from machine lubrication

5. **Loud noise:**
   - From needle action at high RPMs (85–95 dB)

**Guards and PPE recommended:**

- **Finger guards** around needle cylinder openings
- **Light curtains** to stop machine when hands enter danger zone
- **Interlocks** on cylinder covers
- **Two-hand control** for starting
- **PPE:** Cut-resistant gloves, safety glasses, ear protection
- **Lubrication safety:** Automatic lubricators, oil mist extraction
- **Training:** Strict SOP for piecing-up and fault correction

#### Part (b) – 7 Marks
**Explain the safety hazards in non-woven manufacturing, with special emphasis on dust explosion risks in fiber blending and needle-punching hazards.**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Non-woven manufacturing hazards:**

**(i) Fiber blending and opening:**
- **Dust explosion:** Cotton, viscose, and synthetic fiber dust can form explosive atmospheres above **50 g/m³**
- **Mechanical entanglement:** Bale opening, bale plucking
- **Noise and vibration**

**(ii) Carding:**
- **Licker-in roller entanglement** – extremely high RPM
- **Static electricity** leading to sparks (ignition source)

**(iii) Needle-punching:**
- **Needle bar failure** – needles can eject at high velocity
- **Fiber fly inhalation**
- **High noise (95–100 dB)**
- **Vibration** affecting building structure

**(iv) Hydroentanglement:**
- **High-pressure water injection** injuries
- **Slip hazard** from wet floors
- **Electrical hazards** from water near machines

**(v) Thermal bonding:**
- **Hot calender roll burns** (surface temp 180–220 °C)
- **Fire risk** from lint accumulation

**(vi) Chemical bonding:**
- **Solvent/binder vapor exposure**
- **Fire and explosion** from flammable solvents
- **Dermatitis** from binder chemicals

**Control measures for dust explosion:**
- Install **explosion relief panels** sized per NFPA 68
- Provide **dust extraction** systems
- Use **bonding and grounding** for static control
- Maintain **humidity** above 65%
- **Housekeeping** to prevent dust accumulation
- Use **explosion-proof electrical fittings**

**Control measures for needle-punching:**
- **Clamp inspection** of needle boards before each shift
- **Vibration monitoring**
- **Needle ejection guards** (transparent polycarbonate shields)
- **Acoustic enclosures**
- **PPE:** Safety glasses, ear muffs, dust masks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Module 2 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing warp knitting with warp sizing.** Warp knitting is fabric formation (tricot/raschel); warp sizing is yarn preparation for weaving. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Omitting numerical justification.** When asked about shuttle hazards, simply stating "shuttle is dangerous" is insufficient. Always provide kinetic energy or velocity data (e.g., **50 J at 22 m/s**).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Hierarchy of Controls.** When recommending controls, list them in **hierarchy order** (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE). Examiners specifically check this sequence.
>
> 4. **Ignoring dust explosion Kst values.** For non-woven dust explosion questions, you must mention **Kst value** and **MEC**, not just "dust is dangerous."
>
> 5. **Not mentioning PRV testing.** For sizing kitchen questions, examiners look for the phrase **"periodic testing and calibration of pressure relief valves"** – a frequently missed point.
>
> 6. **Confusing noise dose with sound pressure.** Use **dB(A)** and **8-hour TWA**, not raw sound intensity.
>
> 7. **Missing two-hand control.** A very common safety device in modern looms and knitting machines. Always mention it.
>
> 8. **Not connecting engineering control to KTU marking scheme.** The 7-mark sub-parts expect **at least 3 specific control measures**; the 14-mark questions expect **6–8 measures** with categorization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 2: Accident Hazards**

### Sizing Section
- Size cooking kettles operate at **2–4 bar(g)** with surface temperatures up to **160 °C**
- Always install **PRV + rupture disc** in combination
- Steam burns dominate over explosion frequency
- Insulate all pipelines; use **steam traps** to remove condensate
- Provide **splash guards, eyewash stations, chemical-resistant PPE**

### Loom Shed
- Shuttle kinetic energy of **~50 J** is potentially fatal
- **Anti-shuttle-fly guards** are mandatory on conventional shuttle looms
- Shuttleless looms eliminate shuttle hazard but introduce **rapier, air-jet, water-jet** hazards
- Loom shed noise typically **95–105 dB(A)** – requires hearing conservation
- Apply **hierarchy of controls**: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE

### Knitting Machines
- **Circular knitters:** Watch for needle cylinder and dial crush points
- **Flat knitters:** Watch for carriage edge crush
- **Warp knitters:** Watch for needle breakage ejection
- Always install **light curtains** and **two-hand controls**
- Cut-resistant gloves essential during piecing-up

### Non-Woven Section
- **Dust explosion** is the dominant catastrophic risk in non-woven blending
- **Cotton dust MEC:** ~**50 g/m³**, **MIE:** ~**10–25 mJ**
- **Needle-punching:** Needle bar failure can launch projectiles
- **Hydroentanglement:** High-pressure water injury risk
- **Thermal bonding:** Hot calender rolls require fixed guards
- **Chemical bonding:** Solvent fire and dermatitis risks

### Universal Safety Principles
- **Lock-out/Tag-out (LOTO)** before any maintenance
- **Permit to Work** for hot work, confined space, height work
- **PPE standards:** ISI / EN / ANSI certified
- **Reporting:** Every near-miss must be reported
- **Training:** Every operator must be certified before independent operation

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Shuttle velocity | 20–25 m/s |
| Shuttle KE (250 g at 22 m/s) | ~60 J |
| Loom shed noise | 90–105 dB(A) |
| Size kettle pressure | 2–4 bar(g) |
| Saturation T at 2 bar(g) | ~143.6 °C |
| Cotton dust MEC | ~50 g/m³ |
| Cotton dust Kst | ~80 bar·m/s |
| OSHA 8-hr PEL (noise) | 90 dB(A) |
| Steam latent heat at 100 °C | ~2257 kJ/kg |

### Formula Quick-Recall

| Formula | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Shuttle kinetic energy |
| $Q = m L_v$ | Steam heat released |
| $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T}\sum t_i \cdot 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Equivalent noise level |
| $P_{\max} = P_0 + K_{st}(V/V_0)^{1/3}$ | Dust explosion pressure |
| $A = \frac{\dot{m}}{K \cdot P_{\text{set}}}$ | PRV orifice area (simplified) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question says "Discuss the safety measures," always structure your answer as **Engineering → Administrative → PPE**, in that order. This demonstrates understanding of the **hierarchy of controls** and earns full marks from KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scouring, bleaching, dyeing, printing, mechanical finishing operations and effluents in textile processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT756 - SAFETY IN TEXTILE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scouring, bleaching, dyeing, printing, mechanical finishing operations and effluents in textile processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wet Processing in Textiles — An Overview

**Wet processing** refers to the sequence of chemical and mechanical treatments applied to greige (grey/unfinished) textile fabrics after weaving or knitting, to improve their aesthetic, functional, and commercial properties. The four principal wet processing stages — **scouring, bleaching, dyeing, and printing** — along with **mechanical finishing**, constitute the heart of the textile chemical processing industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wet processing contributes nearly **70–80%** of the total water consumption and **60–65%** of the chemical usage in a typical integrated textile mill, making it the single most significant segment from a **safety and effluent management** perspective.

---

## 1.2 Scouring — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Scouring is a chemical wet process in which impurities such as oils, waxes, pectins, proteins, husks, and dirt are removed from textile fibres (especially natural fibres like cotton and wool) using alkaline solutions at elevated temperatures, to improve the absorbency and whiteness of the substrate for subsequent processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Process Conditions:** Scouring of cotton is typically carried out with **NaOH (2–5% owf)** at **90–100 °C** for **45–90 minutes** in a closed vessel.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of scouring as giving your kitchen utensils a deep clean with a strong detergent (like baking soda or caustic soda) before they can be used for cooking. Just as grease and burnt residue must be removed before a clean cooking surface can absorb flavours and seasonings, the natural oils and waxes on cotton must be stripped away before dyes can penetrate the fibre. If you skip this step, your dye job will be patchy — just as a greasy pan cannot hold a proper seasoning.

---

## 1.3 Bleaching — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Bleaching is a chemical oxidative or reductive treatment used to destroy the natural colouring matter (mainly flavonoids and tannins) in textile fibres, producing a **whiteness index (CIE WI)** suitable for dyeing or printing with light shades.

The two principal bleaching agents used in industry are:
- **Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂)** — most common, eco-friendlier.
- **Sodium Hypochlorite (NaOCl)** — cheaper but releases absorbable organohalogens (AOX).
- **Sodium Chlorite (NaClO₂)** — used for synthetic fibres and aramid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Reference Value:** The permissible exposure limit (PEL) for ClO₂ gas is **0.1 ppm (8-hr TWA)** as per OSHA standards. This is critical KTU safety knowledge.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Bleaching is like using lemon juice or hydrogen peroxide on a stained white shirt. The chromophore groups (the colour-causing molecular structures) are essentially "broken" by oxidation, similar to how UV rays from sunlight slowly break down the colour molecules in fabrics left out to dry, making them appear faded.

---

## 1.4 Dyeing — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Dyeing is the process of uniformly applying colour to a textile substrate using dye molecules that form chemical (covalent, ionic, or hydrogen) bonds with the fibre, producing a coloration that is reasonably fast to washing, light, and rubbing.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Dyeing is analogous to marinating food. Just as a marinade penetrates meat fibres through time, temperature, and sometimes chemical tenderising (acid or salt), dye molecules penetrate textile fibres through controlled time, temperature, and chemical auxiliaries (salt, alkali, dispersing agents). The "recipe" (recipe of dyebath) and the "cooking conditions" (temperature-time profile) determine how deeply and uniformly the colour is absorbed.

---

## 1.5 Printing — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Printing is the localized application of dyes or pigments onto specific areas of a textile substrate using techniques such as screen printing, roller printing, or digital (inkjet) printing, to create a designed pattern.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Printing is the textile equivalent of applying a stencil or a tattoo on the skin. While dyeing colours the entire fabric uniformly, printing acts only on the chosen design area, leaving the rest untouched. Think of how a child uses a template to colour specific parts of a drawing — the print paste is the "colour," and the screen/stencil is the template that restricts where it lands.

---

## 1.6 Mechanical Finishing — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Mechanical finishing refers to physical (non-chemical or auxiliary-aided) treatments applied to textile fabrics to alter their dimensional, surface, or aesthetic properties — including calendering, raising, shearing, sanforizing, and stentering.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Mechanical finishing is like grooming — the actual hair is the same, but brushing, ironing, or curling changes how it looks and feels. Similarly, the fibre chemistry is unchanged after mechanical finishing; only its **physical presentation** is altered.

---

## 1.7 Textile Effluents — Definition & Analogy

**Formal Definition:** Textile effluents are the spent processing liquors (and rinse waters) discharged from wet processing operations, characterized by high **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)**, **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)**, **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)**, **colour**, and often extreme **pH (10–13)**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Effluents are the "used bathwater" of the textile industry — containing all the dirt, oil, dye, and chemicals that have been washed out of the fabric. Just as household wastewater cannot be poured into drinking water sources, textile effluents require treatment before discharge because they carry substances that are toxic to aquatic life (dyes, heavy metals from mordants) and deplete oxygen in rivers (high BOD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Effluent Discharge Limits (Indian CPCB norms for textile units):**
> - **pH:** 6.5 – 8.5
> - **BOD₃:** ≤ **30 mg/L** (inland surface water)
> - **COD:** ≤ **250 mg/L**
> - **Total Suspended Solids:** ≤ **100 mg/L**
> - **Colour:** ≤ **400 Pt-Co units** (for some states, "not visible")

---

## 1.8 Summary Table — Overview of Each Process

| Process | Primary Purpose | Key Chemical/Mechanical Input | Typical Output Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scouring | Remove hydrophobic impurities | NaOH, surfactants, heat | Improved absorbency |
| Bleaching | Destroy natural colourants | H₂O₂, NaOCl, NaClO₂ | Whiteness, base for dyeing |
| Dyeing | Impart uniform colour | Dyes, auxiliaries, heat | Coloured fabric |
| Printing | Apply localized pattern | Print paste, thickeners | Designed fabric |
| Mechanical Finishing | Modify surface/dimension | Heat, pressure, friction | Softness, luster, stability |
| Effluent Treatment | Remove pollutants from spent liquor | Biological, chemical, physical | Safe discharge water |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Scouring — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Objectives of Scouring
The objectives, in decreasing order of importance (KTU exam-favourite):
1. Removal of **hydrophobic non-cellulosic impurities** (waxes, pectins).
2. Removal of **natural colouring matter** (partial — bleaching handles the rest).
3. Removal of **sizing materials** (if not removed by desizing).
4. Improvement of **absorbency/capillarity**.
5. Achievement of **uniform whiteness** as a preparation for bleaching/dyeing.

### 2.1.2 Chemistry of Cotton Scouring
Cotton fibre (cellulose, C₆H₇O₂(OH)₃)ₙ contains the following impurities (in approximate % of fibre weight):

| Impurity | % in raw cotton | Solubility in NaOH |
|---|---|---|
| Waxes (fatty acids, alcohols) | 0.4 – 1.0 | Saponifiable/Emulsifiable |
| Pectins (polygalacturonic acid) | 0.4 – 1.2 | Soluble (forms pectates) |
| Proteins | 1.0 – 1.5 | Soluble |
| Minerals (ash) | 0.7 – 1.6 | Partially soluble |
| Natural pigments | Trace | Partially soluble |

### 2.1.3 Sequential Steps (Bulleted Logic)
1. **Wetting** — surfactant reduces surface tension of the bath, allowing the liquor to penetrate the fibre capillaries.
2. **Saponification** — NaOH reacts with fatty acids/waxes to form water-soluble soaps.

$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

3. **Emulsification** — remaining unsaponifiable oils are dispersed as fine droplets with the help of surfactants.
4. **Hydrolysis of pectins** — pectin methyl ester groups are hydrolyzed, releasing soluble polygalacturonates.
5. **Removal of proteins** — proteins dissolve in hot alkali as sodium salts of amino acids.
6. **Washing** — multiple hot/cold rinses remove dissolved impurities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is NaOH used?** NaOH is preferred because it is a **strong alkali (high pH ~ 13–14)** that effectively saponifies waxes, swells cellulose to open structure, and is relatively cheap. Sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃, pH ~ 11.5) is milder and used for wool scouring.

### 2.1.4 Scouring of Wool (Special Case)
Wool is a **protein fibre** and cannot tolerate strong NaOH (it dissolves). Therefore:
- **Raw wool scouring** is done with **neutral detergents + Na₂CO₃** at **45–55 °C**.
- A typical recipe: 0.5–1.0% non-ionic detergent + 2% Na₂CO₃ owf.

---

## 2.2 Bleaching — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Mechanism of Hydrogen Peroxide Bleaching
H₂O₂ in alkaline conditions (pH 10.5–11.5, stabilized by sodium silicate or organic stabilizers) generates the **perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻)**, which is the active bleaching species:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{OH}^- \longleftrightarrow \text{HOO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The HOO⁻ anion attacks the **conjugated double bonds** (chromophores) of natural pigments, breaking them into smaller, colourless fragments — primarily through electrophilic addition.

### 2.2.2 Bleaching with Sodium Hypochlorite
NaOCl generates **HOCl**, which then produces nascent oxygen:

$$\text{NaOCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longleftrightarrow \text{HOCl} + \text{NaOH}$$

$$\text{HOCl} \longrightarrow \text{HCl} + [\text{O}]$$

The nascent oxygen [O] attacks and destroys chromophores but can also attack cellulose, causing **oxycellulose** formation (a strength loss problem — a KTU safety concern).

### 2.2.3 Bleaching Process Conditions

| Bleaching Agent | Concentration | Temperature | Time | pH |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H₂O₂ (35%) | 2–5 g/L | 90–100 °C | 60–90 min | 10.5–11.5 |
| NaOCl (active Cl₂ 5%) | 2–5 g/L | 30–40 °C | 30–60 min | 9.5–10.5 |
| NaClO₂ | 2–5 g/L | 85–95 °C | 60–90 min | 3.5–4.0 |

### 2.2.4 Safety Concerns in Bleaching
- **H₂O₂** at >50% concentration is a strong oxidizer — store away from organics and reducing agents.
- **NaOCl + acids** release toxic **Cl₂ gas** — never mix.
- **NaClO₂** releases **ClO₂** gas (toxic, explosive above 10% in air) — must be used in closed vessels with scrubbers.

---

## 2.3 Dyeing — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Classification of Dyes (KTU Board Favourite)

| Dye Class | Fibre Application | Bonding Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactive | Cellulosics (cotton) | Covalent | Procion Red MX-5B |
| Vat | Cellulosics | Insoluble pigment inside fibre | Indigo |
| Direct | Cellulosics | Hydrogen + van der Waals | Congo Red |
| Acid | Protein, polyamide | Ionic (salt linkage) | Acid Orange II |
| Disperse | Polyester | Solid solution | Disperse Blue 183 |
| Basic | Acrylic, modified polyester | Ionic | Basic Blue 9 |
| Sulphur | Cellulosics (cheap blacks) | Disulphide linkage | Sulphur Black 1 |

### 2.3.2 Stages of Dyeing (Universal — Triphasic Model)
1. **Diffusion** of dye from bulk liquor to fibre surface (boundary layer transport).
2. **Adsorption** of dye onto the fibre outer surface.
3. **Absorption/Diffusion** of dye into the fibre interior.
4. **Fixation** via chemical bonding (for reactive dyes) or physical entrapment (for disperse/vat).

### 2.3.3 Dyeing of Cotton with Reactive Dyes — Chemistry
Reactive dyes contain a reactive group (e.g., vinyl sulphone, monochlorotriazine) that reacts with the –OH of cellulose under alkaline conditions:

$$\text{Dye–SO}_2\text{–CH=CH}_2 + \text{Cell–OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3} \text{Dye–SO}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–Cell}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Salt (NaCl or Glauber salt Na₂SO₄·10H₂O)** is added to the dyebath in reactive dyeing to **enhance exhaustion** by neutralizing the negative surface charge of cotton in water, reducing the electrostatic repulsion between dye anion and fibre.

### 2.3.4 Exhaustion and Fixation Calculations

The **Exhaustion percentage (E%)** is defined as:

$$E\% = \frac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0} \times 100$$

Where $C_0$ = initial dye concentration and $C_1$ = final dye concentration in the bath.

The **Fixation percentage (F%)** is:

$$F\% = \frac{\text{Dye chemically fixed on fibre}}{\text{Total dye applied}} \times 100$$

The **Total Fixation Yield (TFY)** combines both:

$$\text{TFY} = \frac{E\% \times F\%}{10000} \times 100$$

---

## 2.4 Printing — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 Components of Print Paste
- **Dye/Pigment** — colour-imparting agent.
- **Thickener** — provides viscosity (sodium alginate, guar gum, CMC).
- **Fixing agent** — alkali or acid.
- **Wetting agent** — ensures penetration.
- **Hygroscopic agent** — glycerine/urea — keeps paste moist during steaming.
- **Solvent** — water (for most printing; organic for some disperse prints).

### 2.4.2 Common Printing Techniques
1. **Screen (Flat-bed) Printing** — most common; uses a mesh screen with stencil.
2. **Rotary Screen Printing** — high speed, continuous.
3. **Roller Printing** — older, copper engraved rollers.
4. **Digital Inkjet Printing** — modern, low waste, no screens required.
5. **Block Printing** — manual, used in craft textiles.

### 2.4.3 Post-Printing Steps
After printing, the fabric undergoes:
1. **Drying** — removes water, prevents bleeding.
2. **Steaming** — fixes dye (for reactive/steam-print: 102 °C, 8–12 min saturated steam).
3. **Washing** — removes thickener and unfixed dye.
4. **Drying** — final finish.

---

## 2.5 Mechanical Finishing — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 Classification

| Finishing Type | Machine | Effect on Fabric |
|---|---|---|
| Calendering | Calender (heated rollers) | Smoothness, luster, glazing |
| Raising (Brushing) | Raising roller with wire/card clothing | Softness, napped surface, warmth |
| Shearing/Cropping | Shearing cylinder with spiral blades | Uniform pile height |
| Sanforizing | Sanforizer (rubber belt compactor) | Pre-shrinkage control (<1% residual) |
| Stentering | Stenter frame (pins/clips on chain) | Width setting, drying, thermal setting |
| Decatizing | Decatizing machine | Luster, dimensional stability (wool) |
| Compacting | Compact machine (felt + roller) | Hand-feel, softness |
| Emerizing/Sueding | Emerizing rollers (sandpaper-like) | Peach-skin surface |
| Anti-crease (mechanical) | Tenter + resin | Crease recovery angle |

### 2.5.2 Safety Concerns in Mechanical Finishing
- **Stenter frame** has chain-and-pin/clamp mechanisms — **entrapment hazards**.
- **Calender rollers** at 150–200 °C — **burn hazards**.
- **Shearing blades** — **cut hazards**; rotating blades must be guarded.
- **Sanforizing rubber belt** — nip point hazards.

---

## 2.6 Textile Effluents — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.6.1 Characteristics of Combined Textile Effluent

| Parameter | Typical Range (Untreated) | Statutory Limit (CPCB) |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 9.0 – 13.0 | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| BOD₃ (mg/L) | 800 – 2500 | ≤ 30 |
| COD (mg/L) | 1500 – 5000 | ≤ 250 |
| TDS (mg/L) | 2000 – 8000 | ≤ 2100 |
| TSS (mg/L) | 200 – 700 | ≤ 100 |
| Colour (Pt-Co) | Visible/dark | Not visible (varies) |
| Temperature | 35 – 60 °C | ≤ 40 °C at discharge |
| Heavy metals (mg/L) | Cr 0.5 – 5; Cu 0.1 – 1 | Cr ≤ 2; Cu ≤ 3 |

### 2.6.2 Effluent Treatment Sequence (ETP)
1. **Equalization** — uniform pH, flow.
2. **Neutralization** — acid/alkali adjustment.
3. **Coagulation–Flocculation** — alum, polyaluminium chloride (PAC), polyelectrolytes.
4. **Sedimentation/Primary Clarification** — removes suspended solids.
5. **Biological Treatment (Activated Sludge / Aerated Lagoon / UASB)** — reduces BOD.
6. **Tertiary Treatment** — sand filtration, activated carbon, ozone, UV.
7. **Sludge Handling** — dewatering, drying, disposal (or landfill/incineration).

### 2.6.3 Pollution Load Calculation (KTU Formula)

The pollution load from a textile unit is calculated as:

$$P = Q \times C$$

Where $P$ = pollutant load (kg/day), $Q$ = effluent flow (m³/day), and $C$ = pollutant concentration (kg/m³ = g/L).

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If a unit discharges 500 m³/day of effluent with COD = 1500 mg/L = 1.5 g/L = 0.0015 kg/L = 1.5 kg/m³, then the COD load = $500 \times 1.5 = 750$ kg/day. The ETP must remove this load to meet ≤ 250 mg/L, i.e., 125 kg/day discharged, removing 625 kg/day COD.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the consolidated formula sheet for the topic. **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` inside any cell that needs an absolute-value-like expression**, to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exhaustion % | $E\% = \dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0} \times 100$ | $C_0, C_1$ in g/L |
| 2 | Fixation % | $F\% = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Dye}}{\text{Total Dye}} \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Total Fixation Yield | $\text{TFY} = \dfrac{E\% \times F\%}{10000} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Pollution Load | $P = Q \times C$ | $Q$ in m³/day, $C$ in kg/m³, $P$ in kg/day |
| 5 | BOD Dilution Factor | $\text{BOD}_5 = (D_1 - D_2) - \dfrac{(B_1 - B_2)(1 - f)}{f}$ | where $f$ = sample fraction, $D_1, D_2$ = DO initial/final |
| 6 | Liquor Ratio (LR) | $\text{LR} = \dfrac{\text{Volume of bath (L)}}{\text{Weight of fabric (kg)}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Wash Fastness Grade | Grey Scale 1–5 (5 = best) | ISO 105 C01–C06 |
| 8 | Light Fastness | Blue Wool Standard 1–8 (8 = best) | ISO 105 B02 |
| 9 | pOH from pH | $\text{pOH} = 14 - \text{pH}$ | at 25 °C |
| 10 | Strength Retention % | $S\% = \dfrac{\text{After treatment tensile}}{\text{Before treatment tensile}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Saponification Value | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56}{W}$ | $W$ = sample wt (g) |
| 12 | Whiteness Index (Berger) | $\text{WI} = 3.011 \cdot Z^3 - 3.\,\allowbreak 0\,\allowbreak 1\,\allowbreak 7\,\allowbreak 4\,\allowbreak 8\,\allowbreak 0$ (from CIE) | CIE Lab coordinates |
| 13 | Whiteness Index (CIE) | $\text{WI} = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$ | tristimulus |
| 14 | Adsorption Isotherm (Langmuir) | $\dfrac{C_e}{q_e} = \dfrac{1}{q_m b} + \dfrac{C_e}{q_m}$ | $q_e$ = mg dye/g adsorbent |
| 15 | Freundlich Isotherm | $\log q_e = \log K_F + \dfrac{1}{n}\log C_e$ | empirical |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used in textile process houses to (a) audit dyebath efficiency, (b) design ETP capacity, (c) certify compliance with pollution control boards, and (d) predict and optimize dye/chemical consumption for cost reduction.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reactive Dye Total Fixation Yield (TFY)

**Problem Statement:** A cotton dyeing batch uses 4.0% owf (on weight of fabric) reactive dye. The initial bath concentration is 2.0 g/L and the final bath concentration (after dyeing) is 0.4 g/L. After soaping off, 20% of the dye initially exhausted onto the fibre is washed off. Calculate the **Total Fixation Yield (TFY)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Fabric weight (basis): 100 kg (assumed)
- Dye applied (% owf): 4.0% = 4 kg dye
- $C_0 = 2.0$ g/L (initial bath concentration)
- $C_1 = 0.4$ g/L (final bath concentration)
- Soaping loss = 20% of exhausted dye

**Step 1 — Calculate Exhaustion % (E%):**

Using the formula from Section 2.7 (row 1):

$$E\% = \frac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0} \times 100$$

Substituting $C_0 = 2.0$ and $C_1 = 0.4$:

$$E\% = \frac{2.0 - 0.4}{2.0} \times 100$$

$$E\% = \frac{1.6}{2.0} \times 100$$

$$E\% = 0.8 \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Conversion logic:** This means 80% of the dye left the bath and migrated onto the fibre. In mass terms: $0.80 \times 4 = 3.2$ kg of dye on the fibre.

**Step 2 — Calculate Fixation % (F%):**

Since 20% of the exhausted dye is washed off during soaping:

$$\text{Dye washed off} = 20\% \text{ of exhausted dye} = 0.20 \times 3.2 = 0.64 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Dye fixed on fibre} = 3.2 - 0.64 = 2.56 \text{ kg}$$

Now, $F\%$ is the fraction of **total dye applied** that is fixed:

$$F\% = \frac{\text{Fixed Dye}}{\text{Total Dye Applied}} \times 100$$

$$F\% = \frac{2.56}{4.0} \times 100$$

$$F\% = 0.64 \times 100 = 64\%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Fixation Yield (TFY):**

Using the formula from Section 2.7 (row 3):

$$\text{TFY} = \frac{E\% \times F\%}{10000} \times 100$$

$$\text{TFY} = \frac{80 \times 64}{10000} \times 100$$

$$\text{TFY} = \frac{5120}{10000} \times 100$$

$$\text{TFY} = 0.512 \times 100 = 51.2\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A TFY of 51.2% means that of every 100 g of dye charged into the bath, only 51.2 g is fixed onto the fabric. The remaining 48.8 g is wasted — primarily in the spent dye liquor, contributing to **effluent pollution load**. Higher TFY (achieved via better fixation auxiliaries, cold-pad-batch methods, etc.) means less pollution and lower dye cost per kg of fabric.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effluent Pollution Load & Treatment Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A textile processing unit discharges 800 m³/day of combined effluent. The COD of the untreated effluent is 1800 mg/L. The statutory discharge limit (CPCB) is 250 mg/L COD. The ETP achieves a COD removal efficiency of 88%. Determine:
(a) The COD load discharged (kg/day) at the outlet.
(b) Whether the treated effluent meets the statutory limit.
(c) The COD load removed (kg/day) by the ETP.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $Q = 800$ m³/day
- $C_{\text{in}} = 1800$ mg/L
- $\eta$ (removal efficiency) = 88%
- Statutory limit $C_{\text{limit}} = 250$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

Since $1$ mg/L $= 1$ g/m³ $= 0.001$ kg/m³:

$$C_{\text{in}} = 1800 \text{ mg/L} = 1.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate inlet COD load (kg/day):**

Using $P = Q \times C$:

$$P_{\text{in}} = 800 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 1.8 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$P_{\text{in}} = 1440 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate outlet COD concentration:**

$$C_{\text{out}} = C_{\text{in}} \times (1 - \eta)$$

$$C_{\text{out}} = 1800 \times (1 - 0.88)$$

$$C_{\text{out}} = 1800 \times 0.12$$

$$C_{\text{out}} = 216 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate outlet COD load:**

$$C_{\text{out}} = 216 \text{ mg/L} = 0.216 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = 800 \times 0.216 = 172.8 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with statutory limit:**

$$C_{\text{out}} = 216 \text{ mg/L} \leq 250 \text{ mg/L} \;\checkmark$$

The treated effluent **meets** the CPCB limit. **Margin of safety:** $250 - 216 = 34$ mg/L.

**Step 6 — COD load removed by ETP:**

$$P_{\text{removed}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}$$

$$P_{\text{removed}} = 1440 - 172.8 = 1267.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Conversion logic:** The ETP removes 1267.2 kg/day of COD. This biological/chemical oxygen demand would otherwise have been discharged into the receiving water body, severely depleting dissolved oxygen and causing fish kills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety & Environmental Impact:** This numerical derivation directly demonstrates why ETPs are mandatory under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** of India and KTU often frames such problems in environmental compliance contexts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Whiteness Index (CIE Method) for Bleached Cotton

**Problem Statement:** A bleached cotton fabric is measured using a spectrophotometer in the CIE Lab system. The tristimulus values are: $X = 81.5$, $Y = 85.0$, $Z = 92.3$. For the standard illuminant D65 and CIE 1931 (2°) observer, the chromaticity coordinates of the illuminant are: $x_n = 0.3127$, $y_n = 0.3291$. Calculate the **CIE Whiteness Index (WI)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $X = 81.5$
- $Y = 85.0$
- $Z = 92.3$
- $x_n = 0.3127$
- $y_n = 0.3291$

**Step 1 — Calculate chromaticity coordinates of sample:**

The sum of tristimulus values is:

$$S = X + Y + Z = 81.5 + 85.0 + 92.3 = 258.8$$

The chromaticity coordinates are:

$$x = \frac{X}{S} = \frac{81.5}{258.8}$$

$$x = 0.3149$$

$$y = \frac{Y}{S} = \frac{85.0}{258.8}$$

$$y = 0.3284$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CIE Whiteness formula:**

From Section 2.7 (row 13):

$$\text{WI} = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$$

**Substituting values:**

$$\text{WI} = 85.0 + 800(0.3127 - 0.3149) + 1700(0.3291 - 0.3284)$$

**Evaluating bracket 1:**

$$0.3127 - 0.3149 = -0.0022$$

$$800 \times (-0.0022) = -1.76$$

**Evaluating bracket 2:**

$$0.3291 - 0.3284 = 0.0007$$

$$1700 \times 0.0007 = 1.19$$

**Combining:**

$$\text{WI} = 85.0 + (-1.76) + 1.19$$

$$\text{WI} = 85.0 - 1.76 + 1.19$$

$$\text{WI} = 84.43$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A CIE WI of **84.43** indicates a fairly well-bleached cotton fabric. Commercial bleached cotton typically has WI between 70 and 90. Higher WI (closer to 100 or above) indicates excellent whiteness, suitable for optical brightener treatment and pastel shade dyeing.

---

| Bleaching (H₂O₂) | H₂O₂ (50%) | Oxidizer fire | Steam explosion | Eye damage | Lung irritation (mist) | Low (H₂O) |
| Bleaching (NaOCl) | NaOCl | Reacts with acids → Cl₂ | Gas evolution | Cl₂ poisoning | Respiratory | AOX, chlorinated by-products |
| Bleaching (NaClO₂) | NaClO₂ | Explosive ClO₂ | Toxic gas | ClO₂ lethal >10% | Chronic lung | AOX |

| Printing | Kerosene (solvent) | Fire/explosion | Inhalation | CNS depression | — | VOC |
| Mechanical Finishing | None/Steam | — | Nip-point injuries | Crush injuries | Hearing (noise) | Thermal pollution |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"List any six objectives of scouring of cotton fabric."**

**Model Answer (6 points, ½ mark each):**
1. Removal of hydrophobic impurities (waxes, fats, oils).
2. Removal of natural colouring matter (partial).
3. Removal of sizing materials (if not removed by desizing).
4. Improvement of absorbency/capillarity of cotton.
5. Achievement of uniform whiteness for subsequent bleaching/dyeing.
6. Removal of pectins, proteins, and mineral matter (ash).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"Differentiate between reactive and disperse dyes in three points."**

**Model Answer (3 points, 1 mark each):**

| Parameter | Reactive Dye | Disperse Dye |
|---|---|---|
| Fibre used for | Cellulosics (cotton, viscose) | Synthetic (polyester, acetate) |
| Bonding mechanism | Covalent bond with –OH/–NH₂ | Solid solution inside fibre (van der Waals) |
| Application pH | Alkaline (pH 10–11) | Acidic to neutral (pH 4.5–5.5) |
