## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style – Module 3, Question Choice A]**

**(a) Explain in detail the chemistry and mechanism of cotton scouring using sodium hydroxide. List the process variables and describe any five safety precautions to be followed during scouring. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chemistry of Cotton Scouring (4 marks):**

Cotton fibre is composed of cellulose ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_3$)$_n$ and is naturally associated with impurities such as:
- Waxes (fatty acids, alcohols, sterols) — hydrophobic.
- Pectins (polygalacturonic acid esters) — hydrophilic but sticky.
- Proteins, amino acids.
- Mineral matter (Ca, Mg, Fe salts).
- Natural pigments (trace).

When cotton is treated with **NaOH (2–5% owf)** at **90–100 °C** for **45–90 minutes**, the following reactions occur:

(i) **Saponification of Waxes:**
$$\text{R-COOH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-COONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The soap (sodium salt of fatty acid) is water-soluble and is washed off.

(ii) **Hydrolysis of Pectins:**
The methyl ester linkages in pectin are hydrolyzed, releasing soluble sodium pectate.

(iii) **Emulsification of Unsaponifiable Oils:**
With the help of surfactants, oils are dispersed as fine droplets.

(iv) **Dissolution of Proteins:**
Proteins dissolve in hot alkali as sodium salts of amino acids.

(v) **Swelling of Cellulose:**
NaOH swells the cotton fibre, opening its structure and improving access to dye molecules.

**Process Variables (1 mark):**
- Concentration of NaOH (2–5% owf).
- Temperature (90–100 °C).
- Time (45–90 min).
- Liquor ratio (8:1 to 12:1).
- Surfactant type and concentration.
- M:L (Material-to-Liquor) ratio.

**Safety Precautions (2 marks — any 5 of the following):**
1. Wear **alkali-resistant gloves, apron, face shield** while handling 50% NaOH lye.
2. Add NaOH **slowly to cold water** (never water to lye — exothermic and violent).
3. Use **closed kier machines** with pressure release valves to avoid steam burns.
4. Ensure proper **mechanical ventilation** to remove alkaline mist.
5. Keep **eyewash stations and emergency showers** within 10 m of scouring area.
6. Train workers in **first aid for caustic burns** — flush with copious water, do not attempt neutralization.
7. Maintain **anti-slip flooring** since spilled alkali is highly slippery.

---

**(b) Discuss the mechanism of hydrogen peroxide bleaching of cotton. Compare it with sodium hypochlorite bleaching. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of H₂O₂ Bleaching (3.5 marks):**

In aqueous alkaline solution (pH 10.5–11.5), H₂O₂ dissociates:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 + \text{OH}^- \longleftrightarrow \text{HOO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The active species is the **perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻)**, an electrophile that attacks the **conjugated double bonds** (chromophores) of natural pigments (e.g., flavonoids, tannins) by:

(i) **Electrophilic addition** to the C=C bond of the chromophore.
(ii) **Ring cleavage** of the aromatic structure.
(iii) Formation of **smaller, colourless fragments** (carboxylic acids, etc.) that are washed off.

The reaction is controlled by a **stabilizer** (sodium silicate, EDTA, or organic phosphonates) to prevent decomposition of H₂O₂ into O₂ + H₂O (waste) and to avoid fibre damage.

**Comparison with NaOCl Bleaching (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | H₂O₂ Bleaching | NaOCl Bleaching |
|---|---|---|
| Active species | HOO⁻ (perhydroxyl anion) | HOCl / [O] (nascent oxygen) |
| pH condition | Alkaline (10.5–11.5) | Slightly alkaline (9.5–10.5) |
| Temperature | 90–100 °C | 30–40 °C |
| Time | 60–90 min | 30–60 min |
| Whiteness achieved | Excellent (WI 80–90+) | Good (WI 70–85) |
| Fibre damage | Low (with stabilizer) | Higher (oxycellulose formation) |
| Effluent impact | Eco-friendly (H₂O as by-product) | Produces AOX (absorbable organohalogens) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Safety | Mild oxidizer — relatively safe | Risk of Cl₂ release on acidification |

**Conclusion:** H₂O₂ is preferred for modern mills due to better whiteness, lower effluent load, and absence of AOX, despite higher chemical cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style – Module 3, Question Choice B]**

**(a) Describe the components of a print paste used in textile printing. Explain the screen printing process in detail with a labelled diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of Print Paste (3.5 marks):**

A typical print paste contains:

(i) **Dye or Pigment** (1–5% w/w) — colour-imparting agent.
(ii) **Thickener** (2–5% w/w) — sodium alginate (for reactive/disperse), guar gum, CMC, starch ethers. Provides viscosity for sharp print definition.
(iii) **Fixing agent** — alkali (Na₂CO₃) for reactive, acid for acid dyes.
(iv) **Wetting agent** — non-ionic surfactant; ensures penetration into the fabric.
(v) **Hygroscopic agent** — glycerine or urea; maintains moisture during steaming so dye migrates.
(vi) **Solvent** — water (most cases) or organic solvent (carrier printing).
(vii) **Other auxiliaries** — anti-foaming agents, sequestering agents, softeners.

**Screen Printing Process (3.5 marks):**

Screen printing (flat-bed) involves:

1. **Screen preparation** — A mesh (originally silk, now polyester or nylon) is coated with a photo-sensitive emulsion. A positive of the design is placed on the screen and exposed to UV light. The unexposed areas (under the design) are washed away, leaving open pores where dye can pass.

2. **Fabric placement** — The fabric is laid flat on a printing table, stuck down with adhesive.

3. **Printing** — Print paste is poured onto the screen. A **squeegee** (rubber blade) is drawn across the screen under pressure. The paste is forced through the open pores onto the fabric in the design area.

4. **Lift-off** — The screen is lifted, leaving the printed pattern on the fabric.

5. **Repeat for next colour** — For multi-colour designs, separate screens are used for each colour, applied sequentially with intermediate drying.

6. **Drying, Steaming, Washing** — As described in Section 2.4.3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reference:** A labelled diagram of a flat-bed screen printing machine is shown in **Section 4** of these notes (see Section 4.3 for the analogous flow diagram).

---

**(b) Discuss the characteristics and treatment methods of textile effluents. What are the statutory limits prescribed by CPCB for discharge into inland surface waters? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Characteristics of Textile Effluents (3 marks):**

Combined textile effluent (from scouring, bleaching, dyeing, printing) is characterized by:

- **High pH (9–13)** due to alkali used in scouring/dyeing.
- **High BOD (800–2500 mg/L)** due to dissolved organic matter.
- **High COD (1500–5000 mg/L)** due to non-biodegradable dyes.
- **Strong colour** — dye residues.
- **High TDS (2000–8000 mg/L)** — salts (NaCl, Na₂SO₄) from reactive dyeing.
- **Heavy metals** — chromium (from mordants), copper (from certain dyes).
- **High temperature** — 35–60 °C from hot discharges.
- **Variable flow** — depends on production schedule.

**Treatment Methods (3 marks):**

A typical **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** follows this sequence:

| Stage | Unit Operation | Removes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Screening, grit removal | Large solids |
| 2 | Equalization | pH/flow fluctuation |
| 3 | Neutralization (H₂SO₄ or HCl) | Alkaline/acidic extremes |
| 4 | Coagulation–Flocculation (Alum/PAC) | Suspended solids, colloids |
| 5 | Primary Clarifier (settling) | Settled sludge |
| 6 | Biological treatment (Activated Sludge Process) | BOD/COD reduction |
| 7 | Secondary Clarifier | Biomass separation |
| 8 | Tertiary (Sand/Activated Carbon) | Residual COD, colour |
| 9 | Disinfection (UV/Cl₂) | Pathogens |

**Statutory Limits by CPCB (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Limit |
|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 |
| BOD₃ | ≤ 30 mg/L |
| COD | ≤ 250 mg/L |
| TSS | ≤ 100 mg/L |
| TDS | ≤ 2100 mg/L |
| Oil & Grease | ≤ 10 mg/L |
| Chromium (total) | ≤ 2 mg/L |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Concentration Units Confusion:** Students frequently write NaOH concentration as "5 g/L" instead of "5% owf." Remember: **% owf = grams per 100 g of fabric**, while **g/L is concentration in liquor**. For a 1:10 LR, 5% owf ≈ 5 g/L. **State both units clearly.**
>
> 2. **Bleaching Chemistry Error:** Many students write that H₂O₂ bleaching produces "nascent oxygen." This is **incorrect** — the active species is the **perhydroxyl anion (HOO⁻)**. NaOCl produces nascent oxygen, not H₂O₂.
>
> 3. **Reactive Dye Bonding:** Students often forget that reactive dyes bond with cellulose **via the –OH group** under alkaline conditions, and not via ionic bonds (that is acid dyes on protein fibres).
>
> 4. **Effluent Numericals:** Mixing up mg/L, g/L, and kg/m³ is the #1 reason for marks lost. Remember: **1 mg/L = 1 g/m³ = 10⁻³ kg/m³ = 10⁻⁶ kg/L.**
>
> 5. **Process vs. Safety Confusion:** In "safety" questions, students describe chemistry but **forget personal protective equipment (PPE), bund walls, eyewash stations, and statutory norms**. Marks specifically allotted for "safety" are often lost this way.
>
> 6. **TFY Calculation:** A common error is computing TFY as $E\% + F\%$ instead of $\dfrac{E\% \times F\%}{100}$. This is a 2–3 mark deduction per error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### Scouring
- ☑ NaOH (2–5% owf), 90–100 °C, 45–90 min for cotton.
- ☑ Na₂CO₃ + neutral detergent at 45–55 °C for wool.
- ☑ Chemistry: saponification, emulsification, hydrolysis of pectins.
- ☑ Safety: alkali-resistant PPE, closed kiers, eyewash stations, anti-slip floors.

### Bleaching
- ☑ Active species: **HOO⁻** for H₂O₂; **[O]** for NaOCl; **ClO₂** for NaClO₂.
- ☑ pH: 10.5–11.5 (H₂O₂), 9.5–10.5 (NaOCl), 3.5–4.0 (NaClO₂).
- ☑ Whiteness Index (CIE) formula: $\text{WI} = Y + 800(x_n - x) + 1700(y_n - y)$.
- ☑ Safety: never mix NaOCl with acids (releases Cl₂); store H₂O₂ separately from organics.

### Dyeing
- ☑ Reactive → cotton (covalent); Disperse → polyester (solid solution); Acid → wool/nylon (ionic); Vat → cotton (insoluble pigment).
- ☑ Salt (NaCl/Na₂SO₄) used in reactive dyeing to neutralize fibre charge and improve exhaustion.
- ☑ Exhaustion % = $\dfrac{C_0 - C_1}{C_0} \times 100$.
- ☑ TFY = $\dfrac{E\% \times F\%}{100}$ (NOT $E\% + F\%$).

### Printing
- ☑ Components: dye, thickener, fixing agent, wetting agent, hygroscopic agent, solvent.
- ☑ Methods: flat screen, rotary screen, roller, digital inkjet, block.
- ☑ Post-printing: dry → steam → wash → dry.

### Mechanical Finishing
- ☑ Stentering (width setting), Calendering (luster), Raising (nap), Sanforizing (pre-shrinkage), Shearing (pile), Emerizing (peach-skin).
- ☑ Hazards: nip points, hot rollers, sharp blades — guards mandatory.

### Effluents
- ☑ BOD 800–2500, COD 1500–5000, pH 9–13 (untreated).
- ☑ CPCB limits: BOD ≤ 30, COD ≤ 250, pH 6.5–8.5.
- ☑ ETP sequence: Equalize → Neutralize → Coagulate → Settle → Biologically treat → Tertiary filter → Disinfect.
- ☑ Pollution load: $P = Q \times C$ (kg/day) — always state units.

### Safety Across All Processes
- ☑ PPE: gloves, apron, goggles, respirator, anti-slip footwear.
- ☑ Chemical storage: **110% bund walls**, segregation of acids from NaOCl and alkali.
- ☑ Emergency: eyewash, shower, fire extinguisher, MSDS availability.
- ☑ Statutory: Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, CPCB/SPCB norms, OSHA-equivalent Indian Factories Act 1948.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health hazards in textile industry related to dust, fly and noise generated - control measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT756 - SAFETY IN TEXTILE INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Health hazards in textile industry related to dust, fly and noise generated - control measures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of occupational safety in a textile mill, **Health Hazards** are defined as any physical, chemical, or biological agent generated during fibre processing (blowroom to finishing) that has the potential to cause measurable adverse health effects (acute, chronic, or cumulative) to exposed workers. For the scope of Module 3, the three principal physical hazards are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dust, Fly, and Noise** constitute the **"Invisible Triad of Textile Hazards"** because they are not always seen, but cumulatively cause the highest number of occupational diseases in the textile sector worldwide. | Hazard | Definition | Primary Source | |---|---|---| | **Dust** | Solid airborne particulate matter (< 100 µm) generated from fibres, soil, sizing chemicals, and micro-pollutants. | Blowroom, carding, ring spinning, weaving shed, finishing. | | **Fly** | Short, light, fibrous lint particles shed during processing — essentially suspended fibre fragments carried by air currents. | Card room, draw frame, speed frame, ring frame, weaving preparatory. | | **Noise** | Unwanted, intense sound (typically **≥ 85 dBA**) capable of damaging the auditory nerve or causing extra-auditory stress effects. | Looms, ring frames, calendars, tenter frames, stenter machines, humidification plants. | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Spice Rack in a Closed Kitchen" Analogy** 🍳 > Imagine grinding dry chillies in a closed kitchen for 8 hours a day: > 1. The fine red powder that settles on your nose = **DUST** (respirable particles that lodge in alveoli). > 2. The floating tiny threads and lint that stick to wet surfaces = **FLY** (suspended fibrous lint). > 3. The constant loud whirring of the mixer grinder = **NOISE** (continuous high-frequency sound). > > Now scale this kitchen to a 50,000-spindle spinning mill or a 200-loom weaving shed — every worker becomes the "cook" inhaling all three hazards simultaneously for 8 hours a day, 6 days a week. Over months and years, the body's defences break down, producing **byssinosis (Monday fever), occupational asthma, Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL), and hypertension.** --- ### 1.3 Health Outcome Snapshot (Syllabus Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Occupational Diseases Linked to Module 3 Hazards:** > - **Byssinosis (Brown Lung / Monday Fever)** → caused by cotton dust inhalation. > - **Occupational Asthma & Chronic Bronchitis** → caused by cotton, wool, or synthetic fibre fly/dust. > - **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) & Tinnitus** → caused by chronic exposure > **85 dBA (8-hr TWA)**. > - **Hypertension, sleep disorders, and irritability** → secondary effects of noise stress. **Standard Threshold Constants (must memorise):** - **OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for Cotton Dust** = **0.2 mg/m³** (8-hr TWA) for yarn manufacturing. - **WISHA (Washington) Cotton Dust Standard** = **0.5 mg/m³**. - **OSHA Action Level for Noise** = **85 dBA** (TWA). - **OSHA PEL for Noise** = **90 dBA** (8-hr TWA). - **ACGIH TLV-TWA (general industry)** = **85 dBA**. - **Reference Sound Pressure (Pref)** = **20 µPa (micropascals)**. - **Reference Sound Intensity (Iref)** = **10⁻¹² W/m²**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Tri-Hazard Generation Chain (Bulleted Logic) The textile process flow acts as a sequential hazard amplifier: 1. **Blowroom & Mixing** — violent fibre opening releases **dust** and **micro-particles** (trash, leaf, seed-coat fragments). 2. **Carding** — high-speed licker-in and cylinder action generates the **highest density of fly and dust** in any textile operation. 3. **Draw Frame & Speed Frame** — flyer rotation and drafting release fine **fibre fly** into the ambient air. 4. **Ring Spinning & Rotor Spinning** — traveller speed (up to 40 m/s) and rotor turbulence generate noise (**95–105 dBA**) and fly. 5. **Winding & Weaving** — shuttleless looms (water-jet, air-jet) are the **loudest machines** (often exceeding **100–110 dBA**). 6. **Finishing (Calendaring, Stenter)** — heat, steam, and high-RPM rollers generate noise plus chemical dust. --- ### 2.2 Dust vs Fly — A Critical Distinction (High-Yield) | Property | **Dust** | **Fly** | |---|---|---| | Composition | Fibre fragments + trash + chemical residue (sizing, dye) | Pure short fibre lint (cotton, wool, synthetic) | | Particle Size | Typically **< 100 µm** (respirable fraction **< 10 µm**) | Larger (often **100 µm – 5 mm**), lighter | | Settling | Settles slowly, accumulates on horizontal surfaces | Floats, clings to vertical surfaces and machines | | Primary Disease | **Byssinosis, alveolitis** | **Byssinosis, eye irritation, fire hazard** | | Measurement | Gravimetric sampling (mg/m³) | Visual lint index, gravimetric | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB | | 2 | $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB | | 3 | $$I = \frac{P}{A}$$ | Intensity = Power / Area | W/m² | | 4 | $$L_{2} - L_{1} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)$$ | Inverse-square law for point source | dB | | 5 | $$TWA = \frac{C_{1}T_{1} + C_{2}T_{2} + \cdots + C_{n}T_{n}}{8}$$ | OSHA Time-Weighted Average | ppm / mg/m³ | | 6 | $$D = 100 \times \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{C_{i}}{T_{i}}$$ | OSHA Noise Dose formula | % | | 7 | $$\text{Exchange Rate} \Rightarrow \Delta L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{ref}}}{T}\right)$$ | 5 dB doubling rule (OSHA) / 3 dB rule (EU/ISO) | dB | | 8 | $$Q = V \times A$$ | Ventilation flow rate (air changes) | m³/s | | 9 | $$\eta_{\text{duct}} = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{fan}}}$$ | Duct/collector efficiency | dimensionless | | 10 | $$\text{NRR}_{\text{real}} \approx \text{NRR} - 7$$ | OSHA derating formula for ear protectors | dB | > [!NOTE] > **Where Pref = 20 µPa** and **Iref = 10⁻¹² W/m²**. The **3 dB rule** (ISO/EU) states that every **doubling of sound intensity** (or halving of distance) increases SPL by **3 dB**. The **OSHA 5 dB rule** says permissible exposure time is **halved for every 5 dB increase** above 85 dBA. --- ### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > Real-world application: > - **Designing dust extraction ducts** in a card room requires the formula Q = V × A to size axial fans correctly. > - **Acoustic zoning** of a weaving shed uses the inverse-square law to position noise barriers. > - **PPE selection** (ear muffs with NRR 30 dB) requires the OSHA derating formula to estimate real-world attenuation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of OSHA Permissible Exposure Time **Given:** OSHA 5 dB exchange rate, PEL = 90 dBA for 8 hours. **Step 1** — State the OSHA Time-Weighted Average permissible formula: $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}}$$ **Step 2** — For **L = 95 dBA** (i.e., 5 dB above PEL): $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(95 - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{1}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ hours}$$ This means at 95 dBA, a worker may legally be exposed for only **4 hours**. **Step 3** — For **L = 100 dBA** (10 dB above PEL): $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(100 - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4** — For **L = 115 dBA** (25 dB above PEL): $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(115 - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{5}} = \frac{8}{32} = 0.25 \text{ hours} = 15 \text{ minutes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A shuttle loom at **105 dBA** allows only **1 hour** of exposure per day. This single calculation justifies **engineering controls** (sound enclosures, isolation booths) over **PPE alone**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Sound Pressure Level at a Given Distance **Problem:** A carding machine produces a sound power of **P = 10⁻⁴ W**. Find the SPL at **r = 5 m**, assuming a hemispherical (free-field on hard floor) radiation pattern. **Step 1** — Hemisphere surface area: $$A = 2 \pi r^{2} = 2 \pi (5)^{2} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ m}^{2}$$ **Step 2** — Sound intensity at distance r: $$I = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{10^{-4}}{157.08} = 6.366 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ **Step 3** — Sound intensity level: $$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{6.366 \times 10^{-7}}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(6.366 \times 10^{5})$$ $$L_I = 10 \times (5 + \log_{10} 6.366) = 10 \times (5 + 0.8038) = 58.04 \text{ dB}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: OSHA Noise Dose Calculation **Problem:** A worker is exposed to the following noise levels in an 8-hour shift: - 2 hrs at **95 dBA** - 2 hrs at **100 dBA** - 4 hrs at **80 dBA** OSHA Criterion Levels: T₁(95) = 4 hr, T₂(100) = 2 hr, T₃(80) = No limit (below action level). **Step 1** — Apply the dose formula: $$D = 100 \times \left(\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3}\right)$$ $$D = 100 \times \left(\frac{2}{4} + \frac{2}{2} + 0\right)$$ $$D = 100 \times (0.5 + 1.0 + 0) = 100 \times 1.5 = 150\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **D = 150% exceeds the OSHA permissible dose of 100%.** The worker is over-exposed by 50%, triggering mandatory engineering/administrative controls and audiometric testing. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazard → Source → Health Effect → Control | Hazard | Process Source | Health Effect (Acute/Chronic) | Primary Control | Secondary Control | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cotton Dust | Blowroom, Card | Byssinosis, cough, dyspnoea | Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) | Dust masks (P2/P3), humidification | | Wool Dust | Wool carding, sorting | Asthma, "Weaver's cough" | Wet processing, LEV | Respiratory PPE | | Synthetic Fly | Heat-setting, cutting | Skin/eye irritation, dermatitis | Enclosed machines, suction | Goggles, overalls | | Size/Fly | Slasher, weaving | Respiratory irritation | Ventilation, enclosed creel | PPE | | Noise | Looms, ring frames | NIHL, tinnitus, hypertension | Sound enclosures, isolation | Ear plugs/muffs | | Vibration | Calendars, tenter | Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome | Anti-vibration mounts | Limit exposure time | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow: Textile Health Hazard Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Fibre Bale] --> B[Blowroom] B --> C1[Dust Generation
Trash particles] B --> C2[Noise
Beater motors 90 dBA] C1 --> D[Carding Machine] D --> E1[Maximum Fly Density
1000s fibres per m3] D --> E2[Dust Resuspension
Respirable fraction] E1 --> F[Draw Frame] E2 --> F F --> G1[Fly Lint Suspension] F --> G2[Noise 90 to 95 dBA] G1 --> H[Ring Spinning] G2 --> H H --> I1[High Speed Traveller
Fly generation] H --> I2[Traveller Noise 95 to 105 dBA] I1 --> J[Weaving Shed] I2 --> J J --> K1[Shuttle Noise 100 to 110 dBA] J --> K2[Lint Dust from Sizing] K1 --> L[Worker Exposure] K2 --> L L --> M1[Byssinosis
Monday Fever] L --> M2[NIHL Tinnitus] L --> M3[Occupational Asthma] M1 --> N1[Local Exhaust Ventilation] M1 --> N2[Dust Mask P2 or P3] M1 --> N3[Humidification 70 to 80 percent RH] M2 --> P1[Sound Enclosure] M2 --> P2[Ear Muffs NRR 25] M2 --> P3[Acoustic Zoning] M3 --> Q1[Enclosed Carding] M3 --> Q2[Medical Surveillance] M3 --> Q3[Air Cleaning Unit] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Controls (NIOSH Model) ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Elimination
Substitute hazardous fibre] --> R2[Substitution
Use less dusty cotton] R2 --> R3[Engineering Controls
LEV, enclosures, isolation] R3 --> R4[Administrative Controls
Job rotation, shift limits] R4 --> R5[PPE Last Resort
Ear plugs, dust masks] R5 --> R6[Worker Health Outcome] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Ventilation & Dust Extraction Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Src[Dust Source
Carding or Blowroom] --> Hood[Capture Hood] Hood --> Duct[Ductwork] Duct --> Cyclone[Cyclone Separator] Cyclone --> Fan[Exhaust Fan] Fan --> Filter[Bag Filter Unit] Filter --> CleanAir[Clean Air Discharge Stack] Cyclone --> Hopper[Trash Collection Hopper] Filter --> DustBin[Dust Collection Bin] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define **Byssinosis**. List any two symptoms of the disease. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Byssinosis** is an occupational lung disease (also called "Brown Lung Disease" or "Monday Fever") caused by the prolonged inhalation of **cotton, hemp, or flax dust** in textile mills — particularly in carding and blowroom areas. > > **Symptoms:** > 1. Chest tightness and shortness of breath, typically appearing on the **first working day of the week** ("Monday chest"). > 2. Chronic productive cough and wheezing resembling asthma. > 3. Progressive decline in pulmonary function (FEV₁) over years of exposure. (3 marks awarded for: definition 1.5 marks + two symptoms 0.75 each) --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the OSHA **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** for **noise** and the corresponding **Action Level**. **Model Answer:** > **OSHA Action Level = 85 dBA** (8-hour TWA) — triggers hearing conservation program. > > **OSHA PEL = 90 dBA** (8-hour TWA) — legal upper limit. > > Using the 5 dB exchange rule, permissible exposure duration halves for every 5 dB increase above 90 dBA. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice: Answer either Question A or Question B.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Discuss the **sources of dust and fly generation** in a cotton textile mill. Explain how these affect worker health. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **engineering control measures** to mitigate dust and fly exposure in the blowroom and card room. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Sources of Dust and Fly Generation & Health Impact (7 Marks):** **1. Blowroom (1.5 marks):** - Violent opening, blending, and cleaning of cotton bales releases **trash particles** (leaf, stem, seed-coat fragments), **micro-dust**, and **bract fragments**. - Beater and porcupine action disperses these into the air. **2. Carding Room — the *highest dust density zone* (2 marks):** - High-speed **licker-in** (up to 10,000 rpm) and cylinder/doffer stripping generate enormous amounts of **short fibre fly** and **respirable dust**. - Lint index may exceed 5,000 fibres/m³ in poorly ventilated cards. **3. Draw Frame, Speed Frame, Ring Spinning (1.5 marks):** - Drafting rollers and high-speed flyers release **fine fly** into the air. - Traveller speeds (up to 40 m/s) eject fibre fragments. **4. Weaving Shed (1 mark):** - Slasher and shedding action release **size dust** (modified starch) and warp fly. **5. Health Effects (1 mark):** - Byssinosis, occupational asthma, chronic bronchitis, byssinotic syndrome, eye and skin irritation. --- **(b) Engineering Control Measures (7 Marks):** **1. Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) (2 marks):** - Capture hoods fitted directly over beaters, licker-ins, and clearers. - Duct velocity must be **15–20 m/s** to transport dust without settling. **2. Dust Collection System (2 marks):** - Two-stage filtration: **Cyclone separator** (removes bulk fly) → **Bag filter / Dust collector** (removes fine dust < 10 µm). - Cleaned air discharged through a stack ≥ 3 m above the roof. **3. Enclosure of Machines (1 mark):** - Carding machines fully enclosed with transparent acrylic panels. **4. Humidification (1 mark):** - Maintaining **65–80 % RH** suppresses fly generation by adding moisture to fibres. **5. Good Housekeeping & Waste Disposal (1 mark):** - Regular cleaning schedules, sealed waste bags, automated floor sweeping (vacuum-based, not compressed air). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **sources and health effects of noise** in a textile mill. What are the OSHA exposure limits and the 5 dB exchange rule? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Hierarchy of Controls** for noise mitigation in a weaving shed. Calculate the **safe exposure duration** at **100 dBA** as per OSHA. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Sources & Health Effects of Noise + OSHA Standards (7 Marks):** **Sources (3 marks):** - Ring spinning frames: 90–100 dBA (traveller, spindle). - Shuttle looms: **100–110 dBA** (picking motion). - Shuttleless looms (air-jet, water-jet): 95–105 dBA. - Calendaring, stenter, and humidification plants: 85–95 dBA. - Compressed air leaks and pneumatics: 95 dBA. **Health Effects (2 marks):** - **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL):** irreversible cochlear hair-cell damage. - **Tinnitus**, **hypertension**, sleep disturbance, irritability, reduced productivity. - Acoustic trauma from impulse noise (> 140 dB). **OSHA Standards (2 marks):** - **Action Level = 85 dBA (8-hr TWA)** → triggers hearing conservation. - **PEL = 90 dBA (8-hr TWA)**. - **5 dB Exchange Rule:** permissible exposure time is **halved** for every 5 dB increase above 90 dBA. --- **(b) Hierarchy of Controls + Calculation (7 Marks):** **Hierarchy (4 marks):** 1. **Elimination** — replace noisy machines with quieter ones (e.g., soft-pick looms). 2. **Engineering Controls** — sound enclosures, vibration dampers, acoustic barriers between machines, isolation booths. 3. **Administrative Controls** — job rotation, shift limits, posted noise zones. 4. **PPE** — ear plugs (NRR ≥ 25), ear muffs, double protection above 100 dBA. **Calculation (3 marks):** $$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{(100 - 90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ hours}$$ Therefore, a worker may be exposed to 100 dBA for a **maximum of 2 hours per 8-hour shift**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing dust and fly** — they are *not* the same. Fly is fibrous lint; dust includes trash and respirable particulate. Marks lost if not distinguished. > 2. **Wrong OSHA constants** — students often write 80 dBA instead of 85 dBA for the Action Level. > 3. **Skipping the 5 dB derivation** in numerical questions — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for showing the substitution step. > 4. **Ignoring hierarchy of controls** — listing only PPE loses marks; PPE must be the *last* resort. > 5. **Forgetting units** — dust in **mg/m³**, noise in **dBA**, sound pressure in **µPa**. Marks deducted if missing. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Byssinosis** = cotton dust disease; "Monday Fever" pattern is diagnostic. - ✅ **OSHA Cotton Dust PEL** = **0.2 mg/m³** (yarn manufacturing), WISHA = 0.5 mg/m³. - ✅ **OSHA Noise Action Level** = **85 dBA**; **PEL** = **90 dBA**. - ✅ **5 dB Exchange Rule** → halve exposure time per +5 dB. - ✅ **3 dB Rule (ISO/EU)** → SPL +3 dB = double intensity (or half distance). - ✅ **LEV design**: capture at hood, duct velocity **15–20 m/s**, cyclone + bag filter. - ✅ **Humidification 65–80 % RH** suppresses fly and static. - ✅ **NIHL is permanent** — engineering controls preferred over PPE. - ✅ **Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. - ✅ **Reference constants:** Pref = 20 µPa; Iref = 10⁻¹² W/m². - ✅ **Real-world exam mantra:** For 95 dBA → 4 hr; 100 dBA → 2 hr; 105 dBA → 1 hr; 110 dBA → 30 min; 115 dBA → 15 min.

topics.title: Relevant occupational diseases, personal protective equipment - health and welfare measures specific to textile industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT756 - SAFETY IN TEXTILE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relevant occupational diseases, personal protective equipment - health and welfare measures specific to textile industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Occupational Diseases in the Textile Industry — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Occupational Disease (KTU 2024 Definition):** An occupational disease is a health disorder or ailment that arises primarily as a result of the nature of work performed, exposure to hazardous substances, repetitive physical conditions, or a combination of these factors over a sustained period, AND is recognized under the relevant schedule of the Workmen's Compensation Act / Factories Act / ESI Act.

In the textile industry, occupational diseases are specifically those medical conditions whose incidence, severity, and prevalence are statistically and medically linked to the working environment of fibre processing, yarn manufacturing, fabric formation, chemical processing (dyeing, printing, finishing), and garment assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Textile Industry is classified as a HIGH-RISK industry** under Schedule II of the Indian Factories Act, 1948, owing to the multiplicity of hazards: dust, noise, chemicals, heat, humidity, fire, and ergonomic stressors.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Body as a Textile Processing Plant

Imagine a textile machine running 8 hours a day inside a dusty, noisy, chemical-laden room. The human body is analogous to this machine:

- **Lungs** = air intake filter (constantly exposed to cotton dust, fly, fibre fragments)
- **Skin** = outer covering (constantly in contact with dyes, bleaches, sizing chemicals)
- **Ears** = sensitive acoustic sensor (exposed to high decibel loom noise)
- **Muscles & joints** = the mechanical drive (performing repetitive motions)
- **Nervous system** = the control circuit (exposed to solvents like benzene, toluene, formaldehyde)

Just as a machine breaks down if its filters clog, sensors malfunction, or lubricants leak — the human body develops **occupational diseases** when these protective systems fail or are overwhelmed by chronic workplace exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Examiner's Note):** Occupational diseases are DIFFERENT from occupational injuries. Injuries are sudden, one-time events (fractures from machinery). Diseases are slow, cumulative, often latent (appearing years after exposure).

## 1.3 Why Textile Industry is Vulnerable to Occupational Diseases

| Factor | Mechanism of Disease Generation |
|---|---|
| **Raw fibre dust** (cotton, jute, wool, asbestos) | Respiratory system damage |
| **Chemicals** (dyes, acids, alkalis, formaldehyde, solvents) | Skin, lung, liver, kidney, CNS damage |
| **High noise** (looms, spindles run at >90 dB) | Permanent sensorineural hearing loss |
| **High heat & humidity** (dyeing/finishing) | Heat stress, dehydration, heat stroke |
| **Repetitive motion** (sewing, knitting) | RSI, CTS, tendonitis |
| **Vibration** (power looms) | Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) |
| **Poor ventilation** | Accumulation of toxic gases and dust |

## 1.4 Threshold Limit Values (TLV) — Standard Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** is the airborne concentration of a substance below which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects. Expressed in **mg/m³** (milligrams per cubic metre) or **ppm** (parts per million).

Standard textile industry TLVs:
- **Cotton dust (raw):** TLV = **0.2 mg/m³** (raw cotton) per OSHA/ACGIH
- **Noise exposure:** TLV = **85 dB(A)** for 8-hour TWA (Time-Weighted Average)
- **Formaldehyde:** TLV = **0.5 ppm** (8-hr TWA) — ACGIH 2024
- **Cotton dust respirable fraction (yarn manufacturing):** **0.2–0.75 mg/m³**
- **Sulphur dioxide (acid washing):** TLV = **2 ppm**
- **Hydrogen sulphide (desizing):** TLV = **10 ppm**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Occupational Diseases — Detailed Classification

### 2.1.1 BYSSINOSIS (Brown Lung Disease / Monday Fever)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Byssinosis** is a chronic occupational respiratory disease caused by prolonged inhalation of cotton, flax, hemp, or jute dust, especially during the **preparation/spinning** stages (blowing, carding, drawing).

**Pathophysiology (Why and How):**
1. Cotton dust contains **histamine-releasing agents** (bract, bacterial endotoxins from Gram-negative bacteria growing on raw cotton)
2. When inhaled, these release **histamine** in bronchioles
3. Bronchioles constrict → **wheezing, chest tightness, dyspnoea**
4. Symptoms characteristically appear on **Monday** (first working day) and subside during the week → hence "**Monday Fever**"
5. Chronic exposure → irreversible **chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)**

**Stages (Schilling's Classification):**

| Stage | Symptoms | FEV₁ Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| **Grade 0** | No symptoms | None |
| **Grade ½ (Irritation)** | Occasional chest tightness on Mondays | — |
| **Grade 1 (Mild)** | Chest tightness/discomfort on Mondays | Mild |
| **Grade 2 (Moderate)** | Symptoms persist throughout the week | Moderate |
| **Grade 3 (Severe)** | Continuous symptoms, dyspnoea, disability | FEV₁ < 60% predicted |

**KTU High-Yield Point:** Byssinosis is **NOT** caused by cotton fibre itself but by **bracts, bacteria, and endotoxins** in raw cotton dust.

### 2.1.2 NOISE-INDUCED HEARING LOSS (NIHL)

- Generated by **power looms, ring frames, knitting machines, warping machines, jacquard machines**
- Loom noise levels typically **90–105 dB(A)**
- Prolonged exposure (typically > **5 years**) causes **permanent, irreversible damage to hair cells of the cochlea (Organ of Corti)**
- Early sign: **TTS (Temporary Threshold Shift)** → progresses to **PTS (Permanent Threshold Shift)**
- Communication: "**I can hear people speaking, but I cannot understand the words**" — characteristic NIHL pattern

### 2.1.3 OCCUPATIONAL DERMATITIS (Contact Dermatitis)

> [!NOTE]
> Two main types:
> - **Irritant Contact Dermatitis (ICD):** Direct chemical damage (e.g., strong acids/alkalis in dyeing, bleaching)
> - **Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD):** Immune-mediated (e.g., azo dyes, formaldehyde resins, rubber chemicals)

Common textile triggers: **Dermatitis from chromium, nickel, formaldehyde resins, reactive dyes, optical brighteners, sizing agents**.

### 2.1.4 OCCUPATIONAL ASTHMA

Caused by sensitizing agents:
- **Reactive dyes** (especially based on dichlorotriazinyl or vinyl sulphone groups)
- **Formaldehyde**
- **Sizing agents** (proteins, starch derivatives)
- **Enzyme preparations** (e.g., cellulase for bio-polishing)

### 2.1.5 ASBESTOSIS (in Asbestos Textile)

Asbestos is woven/braded for fireproof textiles, brake linings, and insulation. Inhalation of asbestos fibres → **diffuse pulmonary fibrosis**, and is a confirmed human carcinogen (mesothelioma).

### 2.1.6 CANCER (Occupational Carcinogenesis in Textile)

- **Benzene** (used as solvent in some processes) → **leukaemia (AML)**
- **Azo dyes** (with carcinogenic amines like **Benzidine, β-Naphthylamine, 4-Aminobiphenyl**) → **bladder cancer**
- **Formaldehyde** → nasopharyngeal cancer
- **Asbestos fibres** → mesothelioma, lung cancer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The German Consumer Goods Ordinance (Bedarfsgegenständeverordnung) and EU REACH regulation now **ban 22 carcinogenic aromatic amines** in textile dyes.

### 2.1.7 MUSCULOSKELETAL DISORDERS (MSDs) / Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI)

Includes:
- **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS)** — common in sewing, cutting
- **Tenosynovitis** — repetitive wrist motion
- **Tension Neck Syndrome** — poor posture at sewing machines

### 2.1.8 Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)

Caused by prolonged use of vibrating hand tools (e.g., cutting machines, sewing machine trimmers):
- **Vascular:** Vibration White Finger (VWF) / Raynaud's phenomenon
- **Neurological:** Loss of tactile discrimination, tingling
- **Muscular:** Reduced grip strength

### 2.1.9 Other Textile-Specific Diseases

| Disease | Source / Cause |
|---|---|
| **Anthrax (Woolsorter's Disease)** | Inhalation of *Bacillus anthracis* spores from imported wool/hair |
| **Tuberculosis** | Crowded workforce, poor ventilation |
| **Heat stress / Heat stroke** | Dyeing houses, calendar machines |
| **Lead poisoning** | Lead chromate in some dyestuffs |
| **Mercury poisoning** | Antiquated finishing processes |
| **Nystagmus** | Poor lighting (now rare) |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Application in Textile Safety |
|---|---|---|
| **TLV (Time-Weighted Average)** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \times t_i)}{\sum t_i}$ | Average exposure over shift |
| **Exchange Rate (Air changes/hr)** | $N = \dfrac{\text{Volume of clean air supplied (m}^3/\text{hr)}}{\text{Room volume (m}^3)}$ | Ventilation design |
| **Noise Dose (%)** | $D = 100 \times \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \ldots + \dfrac{C_n}{T_n}$ | OSHA noise dosimetry |
| **Time allowed at sound level** | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/5}}$ hours, where $L$ is dB(A) | Maximum exposure time at higher noise |
| **Dose → TWA** | $\text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right)$ | OSHA method |
| **TLV for cotton dust (spinning)** | **0.2 mg/m³** (raw, respirable) | Byssinosis prevention |
| **TLV for noise** | **85 dB(A) for 8 hrs** | Hearing conservation |
| **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)** | **90 dB(A) for 8 hrs** | OSHA legal limit |
| **Heat Stress (WBGT)** | $\text{WBGT} = 0.7 T_{nw} + 0.2 T_g + 0.1 T_{db}$ | ACGIH thermal stress |

## 2.3 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) in Textile Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (KTU 2024 Highlight):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → **PPE (Last line of defence)**. PPE is the *last* and *least preferred* control because it relies on human compliance.

### 2.3.1 Categories of PPE for Textile Workers

**1. Respiratory Protection**
- **Dust masks (N95, FFP2, FFP3):** Filter at least 94–99% of airborne particles ≥ 0.3 µm
- Use in: blow room, card room, ring frame, weaving
- **Air-purifying respirators (with cartridges):** for chemical exposure (acid mist, organic vapours)
- **Supplied-air respirators (SAR):** for high-toxicity processes (formaldehyde, benzene)

**2. Hearing Protection**
- **Earplugs (NRR ≥ 25 dB):** Disposable foam plugs
- **Earmuffs (NRR ≥ 25 dB):** Use when earplugs not feasible
- **Canal caps:** Combination, low attenuation

**3. Eye & Face Protection**
- **Safety glasses** (impact-resistant, ANSI Z87.1 / EN 166)
- **Chemical splash goggles** — for dyeing/bleaching areas
- **Face shields** — for grinding, chemical handling
- **Welding helmets** — for boiler/maintenance

**4. Hand Protection**
- **Cotton/leather gloves** — for material handling
- **Cut-resistant gloves (Kevlar, Dyneema)** — for cutting, slitting
- **Chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile, neoprene, PVC, butyl rubber)** — for handling dyes, acids, alkalis
- **Heat-resistant gloves** — for hot calendaring, stentering

**5. Body Protection**
- **Coveralls / aprons** — protect street clothes
- **Chemical-resistant aprons (PVC, rubberised)** — for dye-bath operators
- **Flame-resistant (FR) clothing** — for boiler, gas-firing areas

**6. Foot Protection**
- **Steel-toe safety shoes** — for material handling
- **Chemical-resistant gumboots** — for wet processing

**7. Head Protection**
- **Industrial helmets (Type 1, Class E, ANSI Z89.1)** — for construction / stores / erection

### 2.3.2 PPE Selection — KTU-Style Decision Matrix

| Workplace | Mandatory PPE | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Blow room | N95 mask, goggles, earplugs | High dust + high noise |
| Card room | N95 mask, earplugs, apron | Byssinosis zone |
| Spinning (ring frame) | Earplugs, light gloves, aprons | Moderate dust, high noise |
| Weaving (power loom) | Earplugs, safety shoes, dust mask | High noise > 95 dB |
| Knitting | Light dust mask, earplugs | Oil mist, low noise |
| Dyeing/Printing | Chemical goggles, nitrile gloves, rubber apron, gumboots, vapour respirator | Chemical splash |
| Bleaching | Acid-resistant gloves, face shield, apron | Strong oxidizing agents |
| Calendaring/Stentering | Heat-resistant gloves, FR clothing, face shield | Hot rollers, surface > 200°C |
| Boiler/Compressor | Helmet, FR clothing, earplugs, safety shoes | Heat, noise, high-pressure steam |
| Garment assembly | Light gloves (cut-resistant at cutting), ergonomic seating | RSI prevention |
| Effluent treatment | Full chemical suit, SCBA, gas detectors | Hydrogen sulphide risk |

## 2.4 Health & Welfare Measures — Statutory Requirements (Factories Act, 1948, India)

> [!NOTE]
> Welfare measures are NOT a luxury — they are **legal obligations** under the Factories Act, 1948 (Sections 11 to 19 and Schedule II/III), and apply to ALL textile factories.

### 2.4.1 Statutory Welfare Amenities

1. **Drinking Water (Sec. 15):** Clean, cool, palatable water, at convenient points. Tested periodically for potability.
2. **Wash Rooms (Sec. 17):** Separate for men and women, with adequate taps, basins, soap.
3. **Toilets / Latrines (Sec. 18):** 1 per 25 males, 1 per 15 females. Must be screened, ventilated, disinfected.
4. **Canteens (Sec. 16):** Mandatory in factories employing > 250 workers. Hygienic, subsidized meals.
5. **Creches (Sec. 17A):** For children of women workers under age 6. Crèche must have attendants, milk, first-aid, separate room.
6. **Rest Rooms / Shelters (Sec. 17):** For workers during breaks, away from work noise/dust.
7. **First-Aid Boxes (Sec. 45):** 1 box per 150 workers; inspected monthly.
8. **Ambulance / Medical Officer (Sec. 45):** Mandatory in hazardous processes.
9. **Overtime Wages (Sec. 59):** Double wages for overtime.
10. **Working Hours (Sec. 51, 54):** Max 48 hrs/week, 9 hrs/day; max 10 hrs including OT.

### 2.4.2 Statutory Health Monitoring

| Examination | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-employment medical exam | At joining | Baseline health record |
| Periodic medical exam | Every 6–12 months | Detect early disease (byssinosis, NIHL, dermatitis) |
| Lung function test (Spirometry) | Yearly | FEV₁ / FVC for respiratory disease |
| Audiometry | Yearly (in high-noise zones > 85 dB) | Detect NIHL early |
| Skin examination | As required | Detect dermatitis |
| Vision test | Annually | Detect occupational visual strain |

### 2.4.3 Industrial Hygiene Engineering Controls

- **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):** At carding, opening, blending, blow room
- **Dust suppression:** Oil mist / water spray in blow room
- **Enclosure of noisy machinery**
- **Sound-absorbing materials** on walls, ceilings
- **Isolation** of boiler / compressor / weaving shed
- **Humidity & temperature control** in spinning
- **Substitution** of toxic dyes/solvents (e.g., water-based dyes)
- **Enclosed dyeing machines** to prevent skin contact
- **Mechanical handling** to reduce manual lifting
- **Ergonomic design** of workstations (correct seat height, footrests, anti-fatigue mats)

### 2.4.4 Occupational Health Surveillance — KTU Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Components of a Health Surveillance Programme (WHO/ILO Framework):**
> 1. **Health Risk Assessment** of workplace
> 2. **Baseline health assessment** of every worker
> 3. **Periodic screening** (spirometry, audiometry, blood tests)
> 4. **Disease recording and notification** (under Schedule III of Factories Act)
> 5. **Rehabilitation and redeployment** of affected workers
> 6. **Termination of exposure** if disease is irreversible

### 2.4.5 Statistical Concepts Used in Occupational Health

- **Incidence Rate:** $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{New cases}}{\text{Person-years of exposure}} \times 1000$
- **Prevalence Rate:** $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Total existing cases at time } t}{\text{Population at time } t} \times 100$
- **Relative Risk (RR):** $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{Incidence in exposed}}{\text{Incidence in non-exposed}}$
- **Sickness Absence Rate:** $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of absent days}}{\text{Total man-days worked}} \times 100$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: OSHA Noise Dose & TWA Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the most commonly asked KTU numerical problems under "Safety in Textile Industry". Students MUST know how to convert noise levels to TWA.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
A worker in a weaving shed is exposed to the following noise levels during a shift:

| Activity | Duration (hours) | Sound Level (dB(A)) |
|---|---|---|
| Power loom running | 4 | 100 |
| Winder | 2 | 90 |
| Maintenance | 1 | 80 |
| Breaks | 1 | 60 |

**Find:** (a) The OSHA Noise Dose (b) The Time-Weighted Average (TWA) in dB(A). (c) Is the exposure above the permissible limit?

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Recall OSHA Reference Table — Permissible Exposure Times

The OSHA standard (29 CFR 1910.95) defines the reference duration $T_i$ for which a given sound level $L_i$ is allowed.

| $L_i$ (dB(A)) | $T_i$ (hours) | $L_i$ (dB(A)) | $T_i$ (hours) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 80 | 32.00 | 98 | 1.91 |
| 85 | 16.00 | 100 | 1.20 |
| 90 | 8.00 | 105 | 0.48 |
| 92 | 6.06 | 110 | 0.19 |
| 95 | 3.83 | 115 | 0.08 |

The 5-dB exchange rule applies: for every 5 dB increase above 85, exposure time is halved.

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Calculate the OSHA Noise Dose (D)

The dose is the sum of the ratios of actual exposure time to permissible exposure time at each level.

$$
D(\%) = 100 \times \left[ \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \dfrac{C_3}{T_3} + \dfrac{C_4}{T_4} \right]
$$

where $C_i$ is the actual hours of exposure, and $T_i$ is the OSHA reference duration for that level.

Now substitute values:

**Activity 1: 100 dB(A) for 4 hours**
- $C_1 = 4$ hrs
- $T_1$ (at 100 dB) $= 1.20$ hrs (from OSHA table)
- Ratio: $\dfrac{C_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{4}{1.20} = 3.333$

**Activity 2: 90 dB(A) for 2 hours**
- $C_2 = 2$ hrs
- $T_2$ (at 90 dB) $= 8.00$ hrs
- Ratio: $\dfrac{C_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{2}{8.00} = 0.250$

**Activity 3: 80 dB(A) for 1 hour**
- $C_3 = 1$ hr
- $T_3$ (at 80 dB) $= 32.00$ hrs
- Ratio: $\dfrac{C_3}{T_3} = \dfrac{1}{32.00} = 0.03125$

**Activity 4: 60 dB(A) for 1 hour** — this is below the 80 dB threshold; contribution is taken as zero (or counted only if integration of partial exposure is required). Standard OSHA practice: levels < 80 dB are **not** counted toward dose.

- Ratio: $\dfrac{C_4}{T_4} = 0$

### 3.1.4 Step 3: Summation and Final Dose

$$
D(\%) = 100 \times \left[ 3.333 + 0.250 + 0.03125 + 0 \right]
$$

$$
D(\%) = 100 \times 3.61425
$$

$$
D(\%) = 361.43\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The dose exceeds **100%**, meaning the worker has been exposed to MORE than the permissible noise level. They are **over-exposed**.

### 3.1.5 Step 4: Calculate TWA from Dose

The TWA formula derived by OSHA is:

$$
\text{TWA (dB(A))} = 90 + 16.61 \times \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right)
$$

Substituting $D = 361.43\%$:

$$
\dfrac{D}{100} = \dfrac{361.43}{100} = 3.6143
$$

$$
\log_{10}(3.6143) = 0.5580
$$

$$
\text{TWA} = 90 + 16.61 \times 0.5580
$$

$$
\text{TWA} = 90 + 9.27
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TWA} = 99.27\ \text{dB(A)}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5: Conclusion

The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) is **90 dB(A) TWA**. Since **99.27 dB(A) > 90 dB(A)**, the worker is over-exposed and corrective action is mandatory:
- Reduce exposure time
- Issue higher-grade hearing protectors
- Apply engineering controls (enclosure, isolation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert the dose to a *percentage* (i.e., multiply by 100) before using the TWA formula. The TWA formula REQUIRES $D$ as a percentage.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Maximum Allowable Exposure Time at a Given Noise Level

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
A textile worker is in a department where the noise level is **98 dB(A)** continuously. What is the maximum permissible exposure time (per OSHA)?

### 3.2.2 Step 1: Use the OSHA Time Formula

$$
T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-85)/5}} \text{ hours}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2: Substitute $L = 98$ dB(A)

$$
L - 85 = 98 - 85 = 13
$$

$$
2^{13/5} = 2^{2.6}
$$

We compute $2^{2.6}$:

$$
2^{2.6} = 2^{2} \times 2^{0.6} = 4 \times 1.5157 = 6.0628
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3: Compute T

$$
T = \dfrac{8}{6.0628} = 1.32 \text{ hours}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4: Conclusion

$$
\boxed{T_{\max} \approx 1.32 \text{ hours per 8-hour shift}}
$$

The worker should not be in this environment for more than ~80 minutes per day.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: WBGT Heat Stress Index for a Dyeing House

### 3.3.1 Problem
In a dyeing section, the following measurements are taken: natural wet bulb $T_{nw} = 30°C$, globe temperature $T_g = 38°C$, dry bulb $T_{db} = 35°C$. Calculate WBGT.

### 3.3.2 Formula (indoor, with solar load)

$$
\text{WBGT} = 0.7 T_{nw} + 0.3 T_g
$$

### 3.3.3 Substitution

$$
\text{WBGT} = 0.7 \times 30 + 0.3 \times 38
$$

$$
\text{WBGT} = 21.0 + 11.4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{WBGT} = 32.4°C}
$$

For heavy work in un-acclimatised workers, ACGIH action limit is **27.5°C** (heavy work) and OEL is **25.0°C**. The shift should be reduced or hydration / cooling enforced.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Exposure → Disease → Control Mapping

| Hazard | Disease | Biological Target | Engineering Control | PPE | Medical Surveillance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cotton dust (bract, endotoxin) | Byssinosis | Bronchioles (lung) | LEV, dust suppression | N95 mask | Annual spirometry |
| Asbestos fibre | Asbestosis, mesothelioma | Lung parenchyma | Substitution, enclosure | P100 respirator, full suit | Chest X-ray, lung function |
| Noise (looms) | NIHL | Cochlea (ear) | Sound insulation, isolation | Earplugs, earmuffs | Annual audiometry |
| Reactive dyes, isocyanates | Occupational asthma | Bronchi | Enclosed dyeing | Half-face respirator with VOC cartridge | Spirometry, IgE |
| Chromium, formaldehyde, azo dyes | Contact dermatitis | Skin | Glove, automation, substitution | Nitrile gloves, sleeves | Skin exam, patch test |
| Benzene, aromatic amines | Leukaemia, bladder cancer | Bone marrow, bladder | Solvent substitution, LEV | SCBA, Tyvek suit | CBC, urinalysis |
| Heat, humidity | Heat stroke, cramps | CNS, electrolyte | Ventilation, AC, hydration | Cooling vests | Acclimatisation protocol |
| Vibration (cutting tools) | HAVS / VWF | Blood vessels, nerves | Anti-vibration mounts, low-vibe tools | Anti-vibration gloves | Cold provocation test |
| Repetitive motion (sewing) | RSI / CTS | Tendon, nerve | Ergonomic design, job rotation | Wrist support | Nerve conduction study |

    TRAIN --> OUTCOME[Effective Protection Compliance Achieved]
```

    WF --> W8[Medical Officer Section 45]

    W1 --> CK
    W2 --> CK
    W3 --> CK
    W4 --> CK
    W5 --> CK
    W6 --> CK
    W7 --> CK
    W8 --> CK
    CK --> OUT[Compliant Healthy Workforce]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Concept Map — Byssinosis Stages and Pathology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cotton Dust Exposure] --> B[Histamine Endotoxin Inhalation]
    B --> C[Bronchial Constriction]
    C --> D[Chest Tightness on Mondays]

    D --> F[Grade 0.5: Irritation on Mondays Only]
    D --> G[Grade 1: Persistent Monday Symptoms]
    D --> H[Grade 2: Symptoms All Week]

    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Redeployment Treatment]
```

    LEV1 --> AR[Administrative: Rotation Time Limitation]

    HP --> A[Audiometric Test: Baseline and Annual]
