The definition is given in **Section 2(m)** of the Act. Once applicable, the Act covers aspects of health, safety, welfare, working hours, and annual leave. The enforcement authority is the **Chief Inspector of Factories** of the respective State. > **[Examiner Note: Award 1 mark each for the two thresholds + 1 mark for Section citation.]** --- ### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **"What is byssinosis and which section of the Factories Act governs its prevention?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Byssinosis** (commonly called *"Monday fever"* or *brown lung disease*) is an occupational lung disease caused by prolonged inhalation of **cotton dust** in textile workers. Symptoms include chest tightness and breathlessness, typically worsening after weekends. Statutory control is provided by: - **Section 14** of the Factories Act, 1948 — mandates effective measures to prevent dust accumulation and inhalation. - **Rule 65 (Kerala Factories Rules)** — limits airborne cotton dust to **2.4 mg/m³** as an 8-hour TWA. - The disease is also **notifiable** under **Schedule III** of the Act. > **[Examiner Note: Mentioning Schedule III fetches the 3rd mark.]** --- ## PART B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** *"Explain in detail the salient provisions of the Factories Act, 1948 relating to **health, safety and welfare** of workers in a cotton textile mill."* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *"Discuss the relevant provisions of the **Indian Boilers Act, 1923** and the **Indian Electricity Act, 2003** as applicable to a textile processing unit."* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A (a): **Step 1 — Scope:** Health, safety, and welfare provisions are covered under **Chapter III (Sections 11–41)** of the Act. **Step 2 — Health Provisions (Sections 11–20):** - **Section 11 (Cleanliness):** Floors cleaned at least once a week; walls of workrooms lime-washed or colour-washed every **14 months**; oil-painted walls every **7 years**. - **Section 12 (Disposal of Waste & Effluents):** Effective arrangements for waste disposal — critical for the dyeing/printing department. - **Section 13 (Ventilation & Temperature):** Adequate ventilation by circulation of fresh air; in cotton textile mills, effective temperature should not exceed **30 °C**. - **Section 14 (Dust & Fume):** All practicable measures to protect workers against inhalation of dust/fume. In a cotton mill, dust extraction ducts at the **blow-room, card-room and ring-frame** are statutory. - **Section 15 (Artificial Humidification):** The State Government may frame rules; humidification water must be pure (Rule 59). Humidification is essential in spinning to reduce yarn breakage. - **Section 16 (Overcrowding):** Minimum **14.2 m³** per worker. - **Section 17 (Lighting):** Adequate natural and artificial light. - **Section 18 (Drinking Water):** Clean, cool water — minimum **5 litres per worker per shift**. - **Section 19 (Latrines & Urinals):** Separate accommodation for sexes; must be accessible. - **Section 20 (Spittoons):** Must be provided and cleaned daily. **Step 3 — Safety Provisions (Sections 21–41):** - **Section 21 (Fencing of Machinery):** Every moving part of the loom, card, ring frame, opener must be securely fenced. Loom shuttle guards mandatory. - **Section 22 (Work on Moving Machinery):** Women & young persons (14–18) cannot clean/lubricate/adjust moving machinery. - **Section 23 (Young Persons on Dangerous Machines):** Persons under 18 not employed on dangerous machines. - **Section 26 (Hoists & Lifts):** Used in cotton godowns, must be inspected annually. - **Section 27 (Revolving Machinery):** Safe speed permanently marked. - **Section 28 (Pressure Plant):** Steam digesters, boilers under Indian Boilers Act. - **Section 32 (Protection of Eyes):** Goggles mandatory in ginning, wool processing. - **Section 36 (Explosive Dust/Gas):** Precautions against cotton fly + sparks in blow-room and card-room — fire-fighting equipment mandatory. - **Section 40 (Safety Officer):** Mandatory appointment in hazardous textile units with 1,000+ workers. - **Section 41 (Notification of Accidents):** Within **24 hours**. **Step 4 — Welfare Provisions (Sections 42–50):** - Washing facilities, bathing places, sitting arrangements, first-aid boxes (**1 per 100 workers or part thereof**), crèches for children of female workers, canteens in units employing 250+ workers. > **[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 2 marks (Health) + 3 marks (Safety) + 2 marks (Welfare). Section numbers must be cited.]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A (b): **Step 1 — Indian Boilers Act, 1923:** - **Applicability:** All steam boilers exceeding **22.75 litres capacity** (or steam pressure > 1 kg/cm²) come under this Act. - **Authority:** The **Boiler Inspector** (Chief Inspector of Boilers under Section 5). - **Registration:** Every boiler must be registered under **Section 7**. A **certificate of registration** is granted after a hydraulic pressure test. - **Inspection:** Annual inspection (internal) + biennial inspection (external). - **Renewal of Certificate:** Every **14 months** for land boilers, **12 months** for marine boilers. - **Application to Textile Processing:** - **Stenter machines** and **dyeing vessels** use steam. - **Sizing machines** have steam-heated cylinders. - **Thermopack machines** for fabric finishing use pressurised steam. **Step 2 — Indian Electricity Act, 2003:** - **Section 53 — Central Electricity Authority (CEA):** Sets standards for electrical installations. - **Section 162 — Penalty for non-compliance:** Up to ₹1 lakh fine. - **Application to Textile Processing:** - **Electric motors** on every loom, ring frame, card. - **Power distribution** panels in the mill. - **Earthing** of all metallic frames mandatory under the **Central Electricity Authority (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2010**. - **Statutory earthing resistance:** ≤ **1 Ω** for industrial installations. - **Insulation resistance:** ≥ **1 MΩ** measured by megger. - Licensed electrical contractor required for HT installations (> 650 V). **Step 3 — Conclusion:** Both statutes operate *concurrently* with the Factories Act. The Boiler Inspector and Electrical Inspector have independent enforcement powers; non-compliance can lead to closure under Section 40-A of the Factories Act. > **[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 3 marks (Boilers Act with section citations) + 3 marks (Electricity Act with safety regulations) + 1 mark for synthesis conclusion.]** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** *"Discuss the **occupational diseases** notified under Schedule III of the Factories Act with special reference to the textile industry."* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *"Explain the salient features of the **ESI Act, 1948** and the **Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923** in relation to a textile worker who suffers a permanent total disablement."* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B (a): **Step 1 — Statutory Framework:** Schedule III of the Factories Act, 1948 lists **29 notifiable diseases**. The employer must notify the Chief Inspector whenever any worker contracts such a disease. **Step 2 — Textile-Relevant Occupational Diseases (mapped to industry process):** | Disease | Cause | Textile Process | |---|---|---| | **Byssinosis** | Cotton dust | Blow-room, card-room, ring-frame | | **Asbestosis** | Asbestos fibre | Fire-proof fabric manufacturing | | **Silicosis** | Silica dust | Cotton dust contains silicates | | **Dermatitis** | Dyes, chemicals | Dyeing/printing | | **Chrome ulceration** | Chromium salts | Wool dyeing, leather finishing | | **Anthrax** | Infected wool/hair | Wool sorting | | **Lead poisoning** | Lead-based dyes | Printing | | **Toxic jaundice** | Chlorinated solvents | Solvent finishing | | **Carbon disulphide poisoning** | CS₂ | Viscose rayon spinning | **Step 3 — Notification Procedure:** - **Section 89** of the Factories Act mandates that the manager of a factory notify the Chief Inspector in **Form 18** within **7 days** of diagnosis by a Certifying Surgeon. - **Section 14 (Precautions against Dust):** Engineering controls — local exhaust ventilation. - **Rule 65:** Statutory cotton dust limit. **Step 4 — Medical Surveillance (Section 13):** Annual health examination by Certifying Surgeon. > **[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 2 marks (statutory framework) + 3 marks (mapping table) + 2 marks (notification procedure).]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B (b): **Step 1 — ESI Act, 1948 (Employees' State Insurance Act):** - **Applicability:** All factories employing **10+ persons** (power) or **20+ persons** (without power) — **same threshold as Factories Act**. - **Wage Limit (2024):** Employees earning ≤ **₹21,000/month** are covered. - **Contributions:** Employer = **3.25%** of wages; Employee = **0.75%** of wages. - **Benefits:** - **Sickness Benefit (Sec 39):** ~70% of wages for up to 91 days/year. - **Extended Sickness Benefit:** For specified chronic diseases. - **Temporary Disablement Benefit (Sec 47):** ~90% of wages for the disablement period. - **Permanent Disablement Benefit (Sec 48):** Paid as life pension (life-time). - **Maternity Benefit (Sec 46):** 26 weeks paid leave. - **Dependent Benefit (Sec 52):** For fatal accidents — life pension to dependents. **Step 2 — Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923:** - **Applicability:** All workmen (lower wage ceiling than ESI), irrespective of factory size. - **Section 3:** Employer liable to pay compensation for injury caused during employment. - **Section 4(1)(c):** Permanent total disablement → compensation calculated using **age-multiplier × monthly wage × 12**. - **Section 4(1)(b):** Permanent partial disablement → proportionate compensation. - **Schedule II:** List of scheduled injuries (loss of limb = 100% disablement). - **Schedule IV:** Age-multiplier table (for age 35 = **55.5** per the 2020 amendment). - **Time limit (Sec 11):** Claim must be filed within **2 years** of the accident. **Step 3 — Application to a Textile Worker (Permanent Total Disablement):** Suppose a weaver aged **35 years** earning **₹15,000/month** loses both hands in a loom shuttle accident. - **Under ESI Act:** He is entitled to a **life-long pension** of ~90% of wages = ₹13,500/month, free medical treatment at ESIC hospital. - **Under WC Act:** Lump-sum compensation = ₹15,000 × 12 × 55.5 = **₹99.9 lakh** (if not an ESI beneficiary) or the ESI pension is in lieu. - **Cross-reference:** A worker **cannot** claim both simultaneously for the same disablement — they are alternative remedies. **Step 4 — Conclusion:** The ESI Act provides *continuous support*, while the WC Act provides *one-time lump-sum relief*. Together they form the **social security net** for textile workers. > **[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 2 (ESI) + 2 (WC Act) + 3 (worked example showing the choice between schemes).]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mistake:** Citing *Section 14 (Factories Act)* for *fire safety*. **Correction:** Section 14 covers *dust/fume*. Fire safety is **Section 36** (explosive/inflammable dust/gas) and **Section 37** (precautions in case of fire). > 2. **Mistake:** Saying the Workmen's Compensation Act is *repealed*. **Correction:** The Act is **still in force** alongside the new Code on Social Security, 2020; until the new Code is implemented by notification, the old Act applies. > 3. **Mistake:** Confusing **Schedule III** (notifiable diseases) with **Schedule II** (WC Act scheduled injuries). Both have *Schedule II* but in *different Acts*. > 4. **Mistake:** Not stating **section numbers**. Each missing section = **−1 mark** in Part B. > 5. **Mistake:** Forgetting to mention **Rule 65 (Kerala Factories Rules)** for cotton dust limit — examiners specifically look for the *Rule number*, not just Section 14. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4** ### A. Statutory Hierarchy (Remember in this order): 1. Constitution of India → Central Acts → Central Rules → State Rules → Inspectorate ### B. Factories Act, 1948 — Must-Know Sections: - **Sec 2(m):** Definition of factory (10/20 worker threshold) - **Sec 11–20:** Health (cleanliness, dust, ventilation, lighting, water, sanitation) - **Sec 14:** Dust/fume (byssinosis link) - **Sec 16:** Overcrowding (14.2 m³/worker) - **Sec 21:** Fencing of machinery (textile-specific) - **Sec 27:** Revolving machinery (safe speed marking) - **Sec 28:** Pressure plant (boilers linkage) - **Sec 36:** Explosive dust (cotton fly + fire) - **Sec 40:** Safety Officer (1,000+ workers or hazardous) - **Sec 41 / 85:** Accident notification (within 24 hours) - **Sec 89:** Disease notification (Schedule III) ### C. Companion Statutes — One-Word Triggers: - **Boiler** → Indian Boilers Act, 1923 - **Shock / Wiring** → Indian Electricity Act, 2003 - **Injury compensation** → Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923 - **Sickness / Maternity** → ESI Act, 1948 - **Effluent discharge** → Water Act, 1974 + Air Act, 1981 + EPA, 1986 - **Hazardous chemical storage** → MSIHC Rules, 1989 - **Sludge disposal** → Hazardous Waste Rules - **Child worker (< 14)** → Child Labour Act, 1986 ### D. Numerical Figures to Memorize: - 10 workers (with power) = factory - 20 workers (without power) = factory - 14.2 m³ = minimum volume per worker - 5 litres/day = drinking water per worker - 24 hours = accident notification time - 7 days = disease notification time - 2.4 mg/m³ = cotton dust limit (Kerala Rule 65) - 90 dB(A) = permissible noise (8-hr TWA) - ₹21,000/month = ESI wage ceiling (2024) - 48 hours/week = maximum work - 30 °C = effective temperature limit (cotton mills) ### E. Common Textile Industry Statutory Triggers: - **Spinning mill** → Sec 13 (humidification), Sec 14 (cotton dust) - **Weaving mill** → Sec 21 (shuttle guards), Sec 27 (safe speed) - **Dyeing house** → Sec 12 (effluents), Water Act, MSIHC Rules - **Boiler house** → Indian Boilers Act, Sec 28 (pressure plant) - **Godown** → Sec 11 (cleanliness), Factories Rules (fire safety) ### F. Mandatory Registers in a Textile Mill: 1. Register of Adult Workers (Sec 27 of Rules) 2. Accident Register (Sec 88) 3. Health Register — Form 17 (Sec 11) 4. Humidity Register (Sec 15) 5. Dust Sampling Register (Sec 14) 6. Boiler Register (Indian Boilers Act) 7. Dangerous Occurrence Register (Rule 110) 8. Leave Register (Sec 79) 9. First-Aid Box Register (Sec 45) 10. Annual Return (Sec 125)

topics.title: Effluent treatment and waste disposal in textile industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT756 - SAFETY IN TEXTILE INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effluent treatment and waste disposal in textile industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — EFFLUENT TREATMENT AND WASTE DISPOSAL IN THE TEXTILE INDUSTRY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Textile Effluent:** Any liquid waste, including process water, wash water, and dissolved/suspended solids, discharged from operations such as sizing, desizing, scouring, bleaching, mercerising, dyeing, printing, and finishing of textile substrates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP):** A coordinated sequence of **physical, chemical, and biological unit operations** engineered to reduce the pollution load (BOD, COD, TSS, colour, heavy metals) of textile wastewater to permissible limits prescribed by regulatory authorities (e.g., Central Pollution Control Board — CPCB, India; US EPA) before discharge into the environment or reuse.

### 1.2 Characteristics of Textile Effluent

Textile wastewater is one of the most complex industrial waste streams because it carries the residues of **fibres, auxiliaries, dyes, and chemicals** mixed in highly variable proportions.

| Parameter | Typical Range (Raw Effluent) | Permissible Limit (CPCB, Inland) |
|---|---|---|
| pH | 4 – 12 | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| Temperature | 30 – 80 °C | < 40 °C |
| Total Suspended Solids (TSS) | 100 – 3000 mg/L | 100 mg/L |
| Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | 1000 – 10000 mg/L | 2100 mg/L |
| BOD₅ (5-day) | 100 – 4000 mg/L | 30 mg/L |
| COD | 300 – 10000 mg/L | 250 mg/L |
| Colour (ADMI units) | 500 – 5000 | 20 (visual) |
| Heavy metals (Cr, Cu, Zn, Pb) | 0.1 – 10 mg/L | 1 – 3 mg/L |
| Oil & Grease | 10 – 500 mg/L | 10 mg/L |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a textile mill's effluent stream as a **patient entering an emergency room** with multiple organ failures:
> - The **Emergency Triage** (screening & grit removal) stabilises the patient.
> - The **General Ward** (primary sedimentation & equalisation) normalises the basic vitals (pH, temperature, flow).
> - The **Intensive Care Unit** (secondary biological treatment) attacks the systemic infection (dissolved organics → bacteria consume BOD).
> - The **Rehabilitation Centre** (tertiary polishing, RO, ozonation) restores near-perfect health before discharge into the environment.
> - The **Organ Bank** (sludge handling) safely disposes of the by-products generated along the way.

Just as a single antibiotic cannot cure a multi-organ failure, **no single treatment step** can bring textile wastewater within statutory limits — hence the **multi-stage ETP** is mandatory.

### 1.4 Sources of Textile Effluent

1. **Sizing/Desizing:** High BOD from starch, PVA, CMC (sizing agents).
2. **Scouring:** Alkaline wastewater with NaOH, surfactants, oils, waxes.
3. **Bleaching:** Chlorine, hypochlorite, H₂O₂ residues; high TDS.
4. **Mercerising:** Strong NaOH (10–25%) with high pH.
5. **Dyeing:** Hydrolysed reactive dyes, salts (NaCl/Na₂SO₄ up to 100 g/L), auxiliaries.
6. **Printing:** Pigments, thickeners, urea, solvents.
7. **Finishing:** Formaldehyde, softeners, flame retardants.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Treatment Architecture

Textile ETP is conventionally divided into **three stages** (sometimes four, with sludge handling):

### 2.1.1 Primary (Physico-Chemical) Treatment

**Purpose:** Remove settleable solids, floating matter, equalise flow & pH, and reduce suspended/dissolved load before biological stage.

- **Screening** — Bar screens (10–50 mm) and fine screens (1–6 mm) remove lint, fabric pieces, threads.
- **Grit Chamber** — Velocity ≈ 0.3 m/s removes sand & inorganic grit.
- **Equalisation Tank** — Dampens hydraulic and pollutant load shocks; residence time = **8 – 24 h**.
- **Neutralisation** — pH correction with H₂SO₄ / NaOH / CO₂.
- **Coagulation–Flocculation** — Addition of **alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃), FeCl₃, PAC (polyaluminium chloride)** with lime; followed by **anionic polyelectrolytes** to form settleable flocs.
- **Primary Clarifier** — Removes 50–70 % TSS, 20–40 % BOD.

### 2.1.2 Secondary (Biological) Treatment

**Purpose:** Biodegrade dissolved organic matter (BOD reduction) using microorganisms.

Two principal modes:

**(a) Aerobic** — Conventional Activated Sludge Process (ASP), Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR), Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR), Trickling Filter.
- Aeration tank: **MLSS 2000–4000 mg/L**, DO 1.5–2 mg/L, F/M ratio = **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹**, HRT = **4–8 h**.

**(b) Anaerobic** — Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB), Anaerobic Filter.
- Used for **high-strength** streams (BOD > 1000 mg/L); produces biogas (60–70 % CH₄).

**(c) Facultative Ponds / Constructed Wetlands** — Low-cost polishing for SMEs.

**Typical removal efficiencies:**
- Aerobic ASP → BOD ↓ 90–95 %, COD ↓ 60–75 %, colour ↓ 20 %.
- Anaerobic UASB → BOD ↓ 60–80 %, COD ↓ 50–70 %.

### 2.1.3 Tertiary (Polishing / Advanced) Treatment

**Purpose:** Remove residual colour, refractory organics, salts, heavy metals — parameters untouched by biological step.

| Process | Target Pollutant | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Ozonation (O₃) | Colour, COD | Oxidation of chromophores |
| UV / H₂O₂ (AOP) | Refractory organics | Hydroxyl radical (•OH) attack |
| Activated Carbon (PAC/GAC) | COD, colour, odour | Adsorption |
| Electrocoagulation | TSS, heavy metals | Al/Fe anode dissolution |
| Membrane: MF/UF | TSS, colloids | Size exclusion |
| Membrane: NF/RO | Salts, dyes | Solution–diffusion |
| Ion Exchange | Heavy metals, salts | Resin exchange |

### 2.1.4 Sludge Handling & Disposal

1. **Thickening** — Gravity / Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF).
2. **Dewatering** — Centrifuge, Belt Filter Press, Sludge Drying Beds (SDB).
3. **Stabilisation** — Anaerobic digestion, lime addition.
4. **Final Disposal:**
   - Landfill (lined)
   - Incineration (with energy recovery)
   - Brick manufacturing (cement kiln co-processing)
   - Composting (if organic, no heavy metals)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** — A regulatory philosophy where 100 % of wastewater is recycled; achieved through **RO + Multi-Effect Evaporator (MEE) + Mechanical Vapour Recompression (MVR) + Crystalliser**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following formulas for ETP design calculations. None contain the pipe character to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times v$ | Effluent flow = Cross-section × Velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time = Volume / Flow | h |
| 3 | $\text{BOD}_L = \dfrac{DO_i - DO_f}{\text{Dilution Factor}}$ | BOD₅ of sample | mg/L |
| 4 | $\text{BOD}_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$ | First-order BOD kinetics (L₀ = ultimate BOD) | mg/L |
| 5 | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{S_0}{X \cdot \theta}$ | Food-to-Microorganism ratio | day⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e}$ | Mean Cell Residence Time (sludge age) | day |
| 7 | $Y_{\text{obs}} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \theta_c}$ | Observed yield coefficient | — |
| 8 | $\text{BOD removed} = Q \times (S_0 - S_e) \times 10^{-3}$ | Mass of BOD removed per day | kg/day |
| 9 | $\text{O}_2 \text{ req} = 1.42 \times \text{BOD removed}$ | Theoretical O₂ demand (aerobic) | kg/day |
| 10 | $\text{MLSS} = \dfrac{M_s}{V}$ | Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids | mg/L |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{(C_i - C_f)}{C_i} \times 100$ | Removal efficiency | % |
| 12 | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{\text{Vol settled (mL/L)}}{\text{MLSS (g/L)}}$ | Sludge Volume Index | mL/g |
| 13 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \cdot J \cdot L}{d^2}$ | Transmembrane pressure (Darcy) | Pa |
| 14 | $\text{F/M} \times \theta_c \times Y - k_d \theta_c = 1$ | Microbial growth balance | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\text{Colour Reduction (\%)} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_t}{A_0} \times 100$ | AOP colour removal | % |

> **Constants used:** k (BOD rate constant) = 0.23 day⁻¹ at 20 °C; Y = 0.6 mg VSS/mg BOD; k_d = 0.06 day⁻¹; 1.42 = oxygen equivalent of biomass (C₅H₇NO₂).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mills in Tirupur (India)** have adopted **Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP)** with **RO + ZLD** after the 2011 Noyyal river contamination judgement.
- **Bangladesh (Dhaka EPZ)** uses UASB + aerobic polishing for denim wet-processing hubs.
- **Italy (Biella, Como)** employs **ozonation + GAC** for residual dye removal — a benchmark for European textile compliance.
- Adoption of **electrocoagulation** is rising in small units because it removes colour with low sludge yield.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order BOD Equation

The biochemical oxidation of organic matter in water follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining oxidisable substrate.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of oxygen depletion.**

$$
\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \, L_t
$$

where $L_t$ = remaining oxygen-demanding load (BOD remaining) at time $t$, and $k$ = deoxygenation constant (day⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate.**

$$
\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k t
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides.**

$$
L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 5 — Note that BOD exerted at time $t$ equals the amount oxidised, i.e., $BOD_t = L_0 - L_t$.**

$$
\text{BOD}_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the classic **Streeter–Phelps** substrate equation used to estimate ultimate BOD ($L_0$) from measured BOD₅ in laboratory respirometers. A textile ETP design engineer uses this to size aeration tanks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Aeration Oxygen Demand (Aeration Tank Sizing)

For an activated sludge unit treating textile effluent:

**Step 1 — State the mass balance on BOD across the aeration tank.**

$$
\text{BOD removed (kg/d)} = Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Substrate removed = oxygen demand + assimilated into biomass.**

$$
\text{O}_2 = 1.42 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot 10^{-3} - 1.42 \cdot \Delta X_v
$$

**Step 3 — For design simplicity, ignore endogenous respiration correction and use the empirical factor:**

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ required (kg/d)} = 1.42 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard oxygen transfer rate (SOTR) using field factors ($\alpha, \beta$, temperature):**

$$
\text{SOTR} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2}{\alpha \cdot F \cdot (\beta C_s - C_L)}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to actual air requirement.**

$$
\text{Air (Nm}^3/\text{h)} = \dfrac{\text{SOTR}}{0.232 \cdot E \cdot \rho_{\text{air}}}
$$

where 0.232 = mass fraction of O₂ in air, $E$ = transfer efficiency (≈ 1.2 kg O₂/kWh for fine-bubble diffusers).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A textile dyeing unit generates $Q = 500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ of effluent. The BOD₅ of the influent is $S_0 = 1200 \, \text{mg/L}$. The required effluent BOD₅ is $S_e = 30 \, \text{mg/L}$. Compute the BOD removed (kg/day) and the theoretical O₂ requirement (kg/day). Assume $k = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C.

**Step 1 — Compute BOD removed.**

$$
\text{BOD removed} = 500 \times (1200 - 30) \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
= 500 \times 1170 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
= 585 \, \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical oxygen demand.**

$$
\text{O}_2 = 1.42 \times 585 = 830.7 \, \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the actual air flow needed (fine-bubble diffuser efficiency ≈ 1.2 kg O₂/kWh).**

$$
\text{Power required} = \dfrac{830.7}{1.2} = 692.25 \, \text{kW (equivalent)}
$$

**Step 4 — Estimate ultimate BOD $L_0$ from BOD₅.**

$$
L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}}
$$

$$
L_0 = \dfrac{1200}{1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}} = \dfrac{1200}{1 - e^{-1.15}} = \dfrac{1200}{1 - 0.3166} = \dfrac{1200}{0.6834}
$$

$$
L_0 \approx 1756 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** Although the *measured* BOD₅ is 1200 mg/L, the *ultimate* oxygen demand is ~1756 mg/L. This must be used in aeration tank design to avoid oxygen starvation during peak loads.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Treatment vs Pollutant Removal Efficiency

| Pollutant | Primary (%) | Secondary Biological (%) | Tertiary (AOP/Membrane) (%) | Overall Achievable (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TSS | 70 | 80 | 99 | 99.5 |
| BOD₅ | 30 | 95 | 99 | 99.5 |
| COD | 25 | 70 | 90 | 96 |
| Colour | 10 | 25 | 95 | 99 |
| TDS | 0 | 0 | 95 (RO) | 95 |
| Heavy metals | 20 | 40 | 99 (Ion Ex.) | 99 |
| Oil & Grease | 60 | 50 | 99 | 99.5 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Complete Textile ETP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Textile Effluent] --> B[Bar Screen 1]
    B --> C[Grit Chamber]
    C --> D[Equalisation Tank]
    D --> E[Neutralisation pH 6.5 to 8.5]
    E --> F[Coagulation Flocculation]
    F --> G[Primary Clarifier]
    G --> H[Equalisation Tank 2]
    H --> I[Aeration Tank ASP]
    I --> J[Secondary Clarifier]
    J --> K[Tertiary Ozonation AOP]
    K --> L[Sand Filter Carbon Filter]
    L --> M[UF NF Reverse Osmosis]
    M --> N[Reuse to Process or ZLD MEE]
    G --> O[Sludge Thickener]
    I --> O
    J --> O
    O --> P[Centrifuge Dewatering]
    P --> Q[Composting or Landfill]
    M --> R[Brine to MEE Crystalliser]
    R --> S[Salt Recovery or Disposal]
```

## 4.2 Stage-Wise Process Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1{Is BOD below 1500 mg per L?} -->|Yes| P2[Use Aerobic ASP Only]
    P1 -->|No| P3[Use Anaerobic UASB First]
    P3 --> P4[Aerobic Polishing MBBR]
    P2 --> P5{Target Colour below 20 ADMI?}
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -->|No| P6[Apply Ozone or Electrocoag]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Apply Activated Carbon]
    P6 --> P8{Target TDS below 2100 mg per L?}
    P7 --> P8
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Discharge to Drain]
    P8 -->|No| P10[Apply Reverse Osmosis]
    P10 --> P11{Target ZLD Compliance?}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[RO plus MEE plus MVR plus Crystalliser]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Permeate Reuse Reject to MEE]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Colour Removal Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUB1[Secondary Clarifier Outlet] --> SUB2[pH Adjustment Tank]
    SUB2 --> SUB3[Ozone Contactor 1 mg per L min]
    SUB3 --> SUB4[UV Reactor 254 nm]
    SUB4 --> SUB5[Sand Filter 0.5 m bed]
    SUB5 --> SUB6[Granular Activated Carbon 1.5 m bed]
    SUB6 --> SUB7[Final Polishing Filter 5 micron]
    SUB7 --> SUB8[Treated Outlet ADMI less than 20]
    SUB3 -. waste ozone .-> SUB9[Ozone Destructor Thermal]
    SUB6 -. spent carbon .-> SUB10[Carbon Regeneration Kiln]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The above flow represents the *tertiary polishing train* for residual dye removal. Ozone degrades chromophores (aromatic rings of reactive/direct dyes), UV + H₂O₂ generates hydroxyl radicals, and GAC adsorbs the by-products. Each stage is sequenced to **minimise operating cost** while satisfying discharge norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any six characteristics of textile effluent and state the typical permissible limits as per CPCB norms for inland discharge.*

**Model Answer (6 key points):**

1. **pH** — Raw 4–12; permissible **6.5–8.5**.
2. **TSS** — Raw 100–3000 mg/L; permissible **100 mg/L**.
3. **BOD₅** — Raw 100–4000 mg/L; permissible **30 mg/L**.
4. **COD** — Raw 300–10000 mg/L; permissible **250 mg/L**.
5. **Colour** — Raw 500–5000 ADMI; permissible **20 ADMI (visual)**.
6. **Total Dissolved Solids** — Permissible **2100 mg/L**.
7. (Bonus) **Heavy metals (Cr, Cu, Zn, Pb)** combined ≤ **3 mg/L**; Oil & grease ≤ **10 mg/L**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
*Differentiate between aerobic and anaerobic biological treatment of textile wastewater.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Aerobic (e.g., ASP) | Anaerobic (e.g., UASB) |
|---|---|---|
| Oxygen | Requires O₂ | No O₂ |
| Energy | High (blowers) | Low |
| BOD removal | 90–95 % | 60–80 % |
| Sludge yield | High | Low (5–10× less) |
| Biogas | None | 60–70 % CH₄ produced |
| Best for | Low–medium strength | High strength (BOD > 1000 mg/L) |
| HRT | 4–8 h | 12–48 h |
| Colour removal | Poor (~25 %) | Poor (~15 %) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** *(Module 4, CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic flowchart, describe the **step-by-step treatment of textile effluent** from screening to tertiary polishing. Mention the function of each unit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle, design parameters, and advantages of the **Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** for textile wastewater.

---

#### Model Answer — (a) [7 Marks]

The textile ETP consists of **three stages** plus sludge handling.

**1. Primary Treatment:**
- **Bar Screening (10–50 mm)** — Removes large lint, fibre pieces, threads.
- **Grit Chamber (v ≈ 0.3 m/s)** — Settles sand, inorganic grit.
- **Equalisation Tank (HRT 8–24 h)** — Dampens flow & pollutant shock loads.
- **pH Neutralisation** — H₂SO₄ / NaOH adjustment to 6.5–8.5.
- **Coagulation–Flocculation** — Alum / PAC + polyelectrolyte; forms macro-flocs.
- **Primary Clarifier (HRT 2 h)** — Removes 50–70 % TSS, 20–40 % BOD.

**2. Secondary (Biological) Treatment:**
- **Aeration Tank (ASP)** — Aerobic microbes oxidise dissolved organics (BOD ↓ 90–95 %).
- **Secondary Clarifier** — Biomass separation; sludge recycled (RAS/WAS).

**3. Tertiary (Polishing) Treatment:**
- **Ozonation** — Chromophore cleavage → colour removal.
- **Sand Filter + GAC** — Residual COD, colour, odour adsorption.
- **UF/NF/RO Membrane** — TDS, salts, residual dye rejection; permeate reused.

**4. Sludge Handling:**
- Thickener → Centrifuge → Landfill / Co-processing in cement kiln.

*(Schematic flowchart should be drawn; refer Section 4.1 above.)*

---

#### Model Answer — (b) [7 Marks]

**Sequencing Batch Reactor (SBR)** — A fill-and-draw activated-sludge system operating in **time rather than space**.

**Operating Cycle (5 Phases):**
1. **Fill** — Influent enters reactor (static or mixed).
2. **React** — Aeration phase; substrate oxidation & nitrification.
3. **Settle** — Biomass quiescently settles; clear supernatant forms.
4. **Decant** — Treated supernatant withdrawn.
5. **Idle/Waste** — WAS removed; next cycle begins.

**Design Parameters:**
- MLSS = 3000–5000 mg/L
- F/M = 0.05–0.3 day⁻¹
- HRT = 12–24 h
- Cycle time = 4–8 h (typical)

**Advantages:**
- No separate secondary clarifier required → **footprint ↓ 30–40 %**.
- Excellent colour and COD removal from reactive dye baths.
- Flexible operation — can be optimised for **batch processing** typical of dyeing units.
- Tolerates **hydraulic and toxic shock loads** (a major textile-industry pain point).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** *(Module 4, CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse — Alternative)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** for colour removal from textile dye-house effluent. Compare **Ozone, UV/H₂O₂, and Fenton** reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A textile dyeing unit discharges 800 m³/day of effluent with BOD₅ = 1500 mg/L. The statutory limit is 30 mg/L. Design an **activated sludge aeration tank** assuming F/M = 0.25 day⁻¹ and MLSS = 3000 mg/L. Estimate the theoretical oxygen requirement.

---

#### Model Answer — (a) [7 Marks]

**Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** generate highly reactive **hydroxyl radicals (•OH)** with oxidation potential **2.80 V** — second only to fluorine.

**1. Ozonation (O₃):**
- Direct attack on conjugated chromophores of azo, anthraquinone dyes.
- Dose: 1–3 mg O₃ / mg dye.
- Colour removal > 95 %; COD ↓ 30–50 %.
- Limitation: short half-life; cost; bromate formation.

**2. UV / H₂O₂:**
- UV (254 nm) photolyses H₂O₂ → 2 •OH.
- Effective for **reactive dye** effluents.
- Operates as a **slurry photoreactor** with TiO₂ catalyst (heterogeneous AOP).

**3. Fenton Process (Fe²⁺ / H₂O₂ at pH 2.5–3.5):**
- Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + •OH + OH⁻
- Colour ↓ 95 %; COD ↓ 60–80 %.
- Forms ferric sludge; requires post-neutralisation.

**4. Comparison:**

| AOP | Colour Removal | COD Removal | Operating Cost | Sludge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| O₃ | Very High | Moderate | High (electricity) | None |
| UV/H₂O₂ | High | High | High (lamp life) | None |
| Fenton | High | Very High | Moderate | High (Fe sludge) |
| O₃/UV | Very High | Very High | Very High | None |
| TiO₂/UV | High | High | Moderate | None (catalyst reuse) |

---

#### Model Answer — (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $Q = 800 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $S_0 = 1500 \, \text{mg/L}$, $S_e = 30 \, \text{mg/L}$
- $F/M = 0.25 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $X = 3000 \, \text{mg/L}$

**Step 1 — BOD removed per day.**

$$
\text{BOD removed} = 800 \times (1500 - 30) \times 10^{-3} = 800 \times 1470 \times 10^{-3} = 1176 \, \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Aeration tank volume from F/M definition.**

$$
\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{\left(\dfrac{F}{M}\right) \cdot X} = \dfrac{800 \times 1500}{0.25 \times 3000} = \dfrac{1\,200\,000}{750}
$$

$$
V = 1600 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical O₂ demand.**

$$
\text{O}_2 = 1.42 \times 1176 = 1670 \, \text{kg/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic Retention Time.**

$$
\text{HRT} = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{1600}{800} = 2 \, \text{days} \; (48 \, \text{h})
$$

**Step 5 — Air required (assume fine-bubble diffuser at $E \approx 1.2 \, \text{kg O}_2/\text{kWh}$, oxygen in air = 23.2 %).**

$$
\text{Air mass} = \dfrac{1670}{0.232} = 7198 \, \text{kg/day} \approx 5600 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{day} \approx 233 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

> **Design summary table:**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Volume V | 1600 | m³ |
| HRT | 48 | h |
| BOD removed | 1176 | kg/day |
| O₂ required | 1670 | kg/day |
| Air flow | 233 | Nm³/h |
| F/M | 0.25 | day⁻¹ |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Textile ETP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing BOD₅ with Ultimate BOD (L₀)** — Always check whether the given BOD is at 5 days or ultimate. Many students forget the conversion factor $L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-k t}}$.
> 2. **Ignoring sludge handling in ETP diagrams** — Full marks for flowcharts require **sludge line** alongside the effluent line.
> 3. **Writing "colour removal" without quantifying** — Always state **% removal** and the unit (**ADMI** or **Pt-Co**).
> 4. **Mixing up units (mg/L vs kg/day)** — When asked for "mass", multiply $Q$ (m³/d) × concentration (mg/L) × 10⁻³.
> 5. **Forgetting the 1.42 factor** — Theoretical O₂ demand must use 1.42 × BOD removed (cell yield correction).
> 6. **Skipping pH range in AOP** — Fenton requires pH 2.5–3.5; ozone requires pH > 7. Wrong pH = **zero marks** for the AOP sub-question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Textile Effluent Treatment & Waste Disposal**

- **Sources of textile effluent:** sizing, scouring, bleaching, mercerising, dyeing, printing, finishing.
- **Three-stage ETP:** Primary (physical-chemical) → Secondary (biological) → Tertiary (polishing).
- **Primary unit operations:** screening → grit → equalisation → neutralisation → coagulation/flocculation → primary clarifier.
- **Biological options:** Aerobic ASP, SBR, MBBR; Anaerobic UASB; Constructed wetlands.
- **Key biological parameters:** MLSS (2000–4000 mg/L), F/M (0.2–0.5 d⁻¹), HRT (4–8 h aerobic), SVI (< 150 mL/g healthy).
- **Tertiary options:** Ozonation, UV/H₂O₂, Fenton, electrocoagulation, GAC, RO, ion exchange.
- **CPCB limits to memorise:** BOD ≤ 30 mg/L; COD ≤ 250 mg/L; TSS ≤ 100 mg/L; pH 6.5–8.5.
- **Sludge path:** Thickener → Centrifuge → Stabilisation → Landfill / Incineration / Co-processing.
- **ZLD = RO + MEE + MVR + Crystalliser** — mandatory for many Indian textile clusters.
- **Master formulas to remember:**
  - $\text{BOD}_t = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt})$
  - $\text{O}_2 \text{ req} = 1.42 \times \text{BOD removed}$
  - $V_{\text{aeration}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{(F/M) \cdot X}$
  - $\text{HRT} = V/Q$
- **AOP hydroxyl radical potential = 2.80 V** — the most aggressive in-situ oxidant.
- **Reactive dye effluent** → high colour + high salt → requires ozonation + RO.
- **Bangladesh, Tirupur, Kanpur, Faisalabad** are global hotspots where textile effluent compliance is heavily scrutinised.
